informal_statement:
Definitions:
- Profile. A \emph{profile} assigns each of the $n$ voters one of the 6 orderings:
$p : \mathrm{Fin}\,n \to \mathrm{Fin}\,6$.
- Pairwise preference functions. $\mathrm{abPref}(k)$, $\mathrm{bcPref}(k)$, $\mathrm{caPref}(k)$ give the
Boolean preference of ordering $k\in\{0,\dots,5\}$ in the $a$-vs-$b$,
$b$-vs-$c$, and $c$-vs-$a$ comparisons, respectively.
- Pairwise preference functions. $\mathrm{abPref}(k)$, $\mathrm{bcPref}(k)$, $\mathrm{caPref}(k)$ give the
Boolean preference of ordering $k\in\{0,\dots,5\}$ in the $a$-vs-$b$,
$b$-vs-$c$, and $c$-vs-$a$ comparisons, respectively.
- Pairwise preference functions. $\mathrm{abPref}(k)$, $\mathrm{bcPref}(k)$, $\mathrm{caPref}(k)$ give the
Boolean preference of ordering $k\in\{0,\dots,5\}$ in the $a$-vs-$b$,
$b$-vs-$c$, and $c$-vs-$a$ comparisons, respectively.
- Boolean hypercube. The \emph{Boolean hypercube} of dimension $n$ is $\{0,1\}^n$,
represented as the function type $\mathrm{Fin}\,n \to \mathrm{Bool}$.
- Sign encoding. The \emph{sign encoding} maps $\mathrm{Bool}$ to $\{-1,1\}\subset\bbr$:
\[
  \sigma(b) \;=\; \begin{cases} 1 & b = \texttt{false},\\
                                -1 & b = \texttt{true}. \end{cases}
\]
- Uniform weight. The uniform probability measure on $\{0,1\}^n$ assigns weight
$2^{-n}$ to each point.
- Vote vectors. Given a profile $p$, the vote vectors $\mathrm{abVotes}(p)$,
$\mathrm{bcVotes}(p)$, $\mathrm{caVotes}(p) \in \{0,1\}^n$ record each
voter's pairwise preference.
- Vote vectors. Given a profile $p$, the vote vectors $\mathrm{abVotes}(p)$,
$\mathrm{bcVotes}(p)$, $\mathrm{caVotes}(p) \in \{0,1\}^n$ record each
voter's pairwise preference.
- Vote vectors. Given a profile $p$, the vote vectors $\mathrm{abVotes}(p)$,
$\mathrm{bcVotes}(p)$, $\mathrm{caVotes}(p) \in \{0,1\}^n$ record each
voter's pairwise preference.
- Boolean function. A \emph{Boolean function} of arity $n$ is a function $f : \{0,1\}^n \to \bbr$.
- Acyclicity. A social welfare function $f$ is \emph{acyclic} if no profile of transitive
voter orderings produces a Condorcet cycle in society's preferences.
A cycle occurs when
$f(\mathrm{abVotes}(p)) = f(\mathrm{bcVotes}(p)) = f(\mathrm{caVotes}(p)) = 1$
(the cycle $a>b>c>a$) or all equal $-1$ (the reverse cycle).
- Odd function. A Boolean function $f$ is \emph{odd} if $f(\lnot x) = -f(x)$ for all $x$.
This models antisymmetry of pairwise preferences in social choice.
- $\pm 1$-valued function. $f$ is \emph{$\pm 1$-valued} if $f(x)\in\{-1,1\}$ for all $x$.
- Walsh character. The \emph{Walsh--Fourier character} associated to $S\subseteq[n]$ is
\[
  \chi_S(x) \;=\; \prod_{i\in S}(-1)^{x_i}
  \;=\; \prod_{i\in S}\sigma(x_i).
\]
The family $\{\chi_S\}_{S\subseteq[n]}$ forms an orthonormal basis for
$L^2(\{0,1\}^n,\mathrm{uniform})$.
- Expectation. The \emph{expectation} of $f$ under the uniform measure:
\[
  \E[f] \;=\; 2^{-n}\sum_{x\in\{0,1\}^n} f(x).
\]
- $L^2$ inner product. The \emph{$L^2$ inner product} of two Boolean functions with respect to the
uniform measure:
\[
  \langle f, g\rangle \;=\; \E[f\cdot g]
  \;=\; 2^{-n}\sum_{x\in\{0,1\}^n} f(x)\,g(x).
\]
- Fourier coefficient. The \emph{Fourier--Walsh coefficient} of $f$ at frequency $S$ is
\[
  \hat f(S) \;=\; \langle f, \chi_S\rangle
  \;=\; 2^{-n}\sum_{x\in\{0,1\}^n} f(x)\,\chi_S(x).
\]
- Correlation function. The \emph{Fourier correlation function} of $f$ is
\[
  \mathrm{corr}(f) \;=\; \sum_{S\subseteq[n]} \hat f(S)^2\,(-1/3)^{|S|}.
\]
For odd $f$, only odd-level terms contribute.

Statement: Acyclicity implies $\mathrm{corr}(f) = -1/3$. If $f$ is $\pm 1$-valued and acyclic, then $\mathrm{corr}(f) = -1/3$.

\emph{Proof sketch:}
\begin{enumerate}
  \item For any $\pm 1$ triple $(a,b,c)$ avoiding the two all-equal cycles,
        $ab + bc + ac = -1$.
  \item Summing over all $6^n$ profiles:
        $\sum_p (f_{\!ab}\cdot f_{\!bc} + f_{\!bc}\cdot f_{\!ca} + f_{\!ab}\cdot f_{\!ca}) = -6^n$.
  \item Each pairwise expected product equals $\mathrm{corr}(f)$, so
        $3\cdot\mathrm{corr}(f) = -1$, giving $\mathrm{corr}(f) = -1/3$.
\end{enumerate}
formal_statement:
import Mathlib

set_option maxHeartbeats 800000
open scoped BigOperators
open BooleanAnalysis
set_option maxHeartbeats 400000
scoped notation "⟪" f ", " g "⟫_𝔹" => innerProduct f g
scoped notation "χ_[" S "]" => chiS S
scoped notation f "̂(" S ")" => fourierCoeff f S

namespace ArrowTheorem
variable {n : ℕ}
abbrev Profile (n : ℕ) := Fin n → Fin 6
end ArrowTheorem

namespace ArrowTheorem
variable {n : ℕ}
def abPref : Fin 6 → Bool
  | ⟨0, _⟩ => false  -- a > b > c
  | ⟨1, _⟩ => false  -- a > c > b
  | ⟨2, _⟩ => true   -- b > a > c
  | ⟨3, _⟩ => true   -- b > c > a
  | ⟨4, _⟩ => false  -- c > a > b
  | ⟨5, _⟩ => true   -- c > b > a
end ArrowTheorem

namespace ArrowTheorem
variable {n : ℕ}
def bcPref : Fin 6 → Bool
  | ⟨0, _⟩ => false  -- a > b > c
  | ⟨1, _⟩ => true   -- a > c > b
  | ⟨2, _⟩ => false  -- b > a > c
  | ⟨3, _⟩ => false  -- b > c > a
  | ⟨4, _⟩ => true   -- c > a > b
  | ⟨5, _⟩ => true   -- c > b > a
end ArrowTheorem

namespace ArrowTheorem
variable {n : ℕ}
def caPref : Fin 6 → Bool
  | ⟨0, _⟩ => true   -- a > b > c: prefer a, so in ca: prefer a = true
  | ⟨1, _⟩ => true   -- a > c > b
  | ⟨2, _⟩ => true   -- b > a > c
  | ⟨3, _⟩ => false  -- b > c > a: prefer c
  | ⟨4, _⟩ => false  -- c > a > b: prefer c
  | ⟨5, _⟩ => false  -- c > b > a: prefer c
end ArrowTheorem

namespace BooleanAnalysis
abbrev BoolCube (n : ℕ) := Fin n → Bool
end BooleanAnalysis

namespace BooleanAnalysis
variable {n : ℕ}
def boolToSign (b : Bool) : ℝ := if b then -1 else 1
end BooleanAnalysis

namespace BooleanAnalysis
variable {n : ℕ}
noncomputable def uniformWeight (n : ℕ) : ℝ := (2 : ℝ)⁻¹ ^ n
end BooleanAnalysis

namespace ArrowTheorem
variable {n : ℕ}
def abVotes (p : Profile n) : BoolCube n := fun i => abPref (p i)
end ArrowTheorem

namespace ArrowTheorem
variable {n : ℕ}
def bcVotes (p : Profile n) : BoolCube n := fun i => bcPref (p i)
end ArrowTheorem

namespace ArrowTheorem
variable {n : ℕ}
def caVotes (p : Profile n) : BoolCube n := fun i => caPref (p i)
end ArrowTheorem

namespace BooleanAnalysis
abbrev BooleanFunc (n : ℕ) := BoolCube n → ℝ
end BooleanAnalysis

namespace ArrowTheorem
variable {n : ℕ}
def acyclic (f : BooleanFunc n) : Prop :=
  ∀ p : Profile n,
    ¬ (f (abVotes p) = 1 ∧ f (bcVotes p) = 1 ∧ f (caVotes p) = 1) ∧
    ¬ (f (abVotes p) = -1 ∧ f (bcVotes p) = -1 ∧ f (caVotes p) = -1)
end ArrowTheorem

namespace BooleanAnalysis
variable {n : ℕ}
def isOddFunc (f : BooleanFunc n) : Prop :=
  ∀ x : BoolCube n, f (fun i => !x i) = -f x
end BooleanAnalysis

namespace BooleanAnalysis
variable {n : ℕ}
def isPmOne (f : BooleanFunc n) : Prop :=
  ∀ x : BoolCube n, f x = 1 ∨ f x = -1
end BooleanAnalysis

namespace BooleanAnalysis
variable {n : ℕ}
noncomputable def chiS (S : Finset (Fin n)) : BooleanFunc n :=
  fun x ↦ ∏ i ∈ S, boolToSign (x i)
end BooleanAnalysis

namespace BooleanAnalysis
variable {n : ℕ}
noncomputable def expect (f : BooleanFunc n) : ℝ :=
  uniformWeight n * ∑ x : BoolCube n, f x
end BooleanAnalysis

namespace BooleanAnalysis
variable {n : ℕ}
noncomputable def innerProduct (f g : BooleanFunc n) : ℝ :=
  expect (fun x ↦ f x * g x)
end BooleanAnalysis

namespace BooleanAnalysis
variable {n : ℕ}
noncomputable def fourierCoeff (f : BooleanFunc n) (S : Finset (Fin n)) : ℝ :=
  innerProduct f (chiS S)
end BooleanAnalysis

namespace ArrowTheorem
variable {n : ℕ}
noncomputable def corrFunc (f : BooleanFunc n) : ℝ :=
  ∑ S : Finset (Fin n), fourierCoeff f S ^ 2 * (-1/3 : ℝ) ^ S.card
end ArrowTheorem

namespace ArrowTheorem
variable {n : ℕ}
lemma acyclic_implies_corrFunc (f : BooleanFunc n) (_hodd : isOddFunc f) (hpm : isPmOne f)
    (hacyc : acyclic f) : corrFunc f = -1/3 := sorry
end ArrowTheorem
title: Acyclicity implies $\mathrm{corr}(f) = -1/3$

informal_statement:
Definitions:
- Profile. A \emph{profile} assigns each of the $n$ voters one of the 6 orderings:
$p : \mathrm{Fin}\,n \to \mathrm{Fin}\,6$.
- Pairwise preference functions. $\mathrm{abPref}(k)$, $\mathrm{bcPref}(k)$, $\mathrm{caPref}(k)$ give the
Boolean preference of ordering $k\in\{0,\dots,5\}$ in the $a$-vs-$b$,
$b$-vs-$c$, and $c$-vs-$a$ comparisons, respectively.
- Pairwise preference functions. $\mathrm{abPref}(k)$, $\mathrm{bcPref}(k)$, $\mathrm{caPref}(k)$ give the
Boolean preference of ordering $k\in\{0,\dots,5\}$ in the $a$-vs-$b$,
$b$-vs-$c$, and $c$-vs-$a$ comparisons, respectively.
- Pairwise preference functions. $\mathrm{abPref}(k)$, $\mathrm{bcPref}(k)$, $\mathrm{caPref}(k)$ give the
Boolean preference of ordering $k\in\{0,\dots,5\}$ in the $a$-vs-$b$,
$b$-vs-$c$, and $c$-vs-$a$ comparisons, respectively.
- Boolean hypercube. The \emph{Boolean hypercube} of dimension $n$ is $\{0,1\}^n$,
represented as the function type $\mathrm{Fin}\,n \to \mathrm{Bool}$.
- Sign encoding. The \emph{sign encoding} maps $\mathrm{Bool}$ to $\{-1,1\}\subset\bbr$:
\[
  \sigma(b) \;=\; \begin{cases} 1 & b = \texttt{false},\\
                                -1 & b = \texttt{true}. \end{cases}
\]
- Vote vectors. Given a profile $p$, the vote vectors $\mathrm{abVotes}(p)$,
$\mathrm{bcVotes}(p)$, $\mathrm{caVotes}(p) \in \{0,1\}^n$ record each
voter's pairwise preference.
- Vote vectors. Given a profile $p$, the vote vectors $\mathrm{abVotes}(p)$,
$\mathrm{bcVotes}(p)$, $\mathrm{caVotes}(p) \in \{0,1\}^n$ record each
voter's pairwise preference.
- Vote vectors. Given a profile $p$, the vote vectors $\mathrm{abVotes}(p)$,
$\mathrm{bcVotes}(p)$, $\mathrm{caVotes}(p) \in \{0,1\}^n$ record each
voter's pairwise preference.
- Boolean function. A \emph{Boolean function} of arity $n$ is a function $f : \{0,1\}^n \to \bbr$.
- Acyclicity. A social welfare function $f$ is \emph{acyclic} if no profile of transitive
voter orderings produces a Condorcet cycle in society's preferences.
A cycle occurs when
$f(\mathrm{abVotes}(p)) = f(\mathrm{bcVotes}(p)) = f(\mathrm{caVotes}(p)) = 1$
(the cycle $a>b>c>a$) or all equal $-1$ (the reverse cycle).
- Unanimity. A social welfare function $f:\{0,1\}^n\to\bbr$ is \emph{unanimous} if
$f(\texttt{false},\dots,\texttt{false}) = 1$: when all voters prefer
alternative $a$ over $b$, so does society.
- Odd function. A Boolean function $f$ is \emph{odd} if $f(\lnot x) = -f(x)$ for all $x$.
This models antisymmetry of pairwise preferences in social choice.
- $\pm 1$-valued function. $f$ is \emph{$\pm 1$-valued} if $f(x)\in\{-1,1\}$ for all $x$.
- Dictator. The \emph{dictator function} for coordinate $i$ outputs the sign of the $i$-th
bit: $\mathrm{dict}_i(x) = (-1)^{x_i}$.  Its unique nonzero Fourier coefficient
is $\widehat{\mathrm{dict}_i}(\{i\}) = 1$.
- Dictatorship. $f$ is a \emph{dictatorship} if there exists a voter $i$ such that
$f = \mathrm{dict}_i$, i.e.\ society's preference always equals voter $i$'s.

Statement: Arrow's Impossibility Theorem. Let $f:\{0,1\}^n\to\bbr$ be a social welfare function that is odd
(antisymmetric), $\pm 1$-valued, unanimous, and acyclic.  Then $f$ is a
\emph{dictatorship}: there exists a voter $i_0$ such that
$f = \mathrm{dict}_{i_0}$.

\emph{Proof:}
\begin{enumerate}
  \item Acyclicity $\Rightarrow$ $\mathrm{corr}(f) = -1/3$
        (Lemma~ArrowTheorem.acyclic_implies_corrFunc).
  \item $\mathrm{corr}(f) = -1/3$ $\Rightarrow$ $\hat f(S) = 0$ for $|S|\ne 1$
        (Lemma~ArrowTheorem.corrFunc_eq_neg_third_of_weight_one).
  \item Degree-1 + unanimous + $\pm 1$-valued $\Rightarrow$ dictator
        (Lemma~ArrowTheorem.degree_one_implies_dictator).
\end{enumerate}
formal_statement:
import Mathlib

set_option maxHeartbeats 800000
open scoped BigOperators
open BooleanAnalysis
set_option maxHeartbeats 400000
scoped notation "⟪" f ", " g "⟫_𝔹" => innerProduct f g
scoped notation "χ_[" S "]" => chiS S
scoped notation f "̂(" S ")" => fourierCoeff f S

namespace ArrowTheorem
variable {n : ℕ}
abbrev Profile (n : ℕ) := Fin n → Fin 6
end ArrowTheorem

namespace ArrowTheorem
variable {n : ℕ}
def abPref : Fin 6 → Bool
  | ⟨0, _⟩ => false  -- a > b > c
  | ⟨1, _⟩ => false  -- a > c > b
  | ⟨2, _⟩ => true   -- b > a > c
  | ⟨3, _⟩ => true   -- b > c > a
  | ⟨4, _⟩ => false  -- c > a > b
  | ⟨5, _⟩ => true   -- c > b > a
end ArrowTheorem

namespace ArrowTheorem
variable {n : ℕ}
def bcPref : Fin 6 → Bool
  | ⟨0, _⟩ => false  -- a > b > c
  | ⟨1, _⟩ => true   -- a > c > b
  | ⟨2, _⟩ => false  -- b > a > c
  | ⟨3, _⟩ => false  -- b > c > a
  | ⟨4, _⟩ => true   -- c > a > b
  | ⟨5, _⟩ => true   -- c > b > a
end ArrowTheorem

namespace ArrowTheorem
variable {n : ℕ}
def caPref : Fin 6 → Bool
  | ⟨0, _⟩ => true   -- a > b > c: prefer a, so in ca: prefer a = true
  | ⟨1, _⟩ => true   -- a > c > b
  | ⟨2, _⟩ => true   -- b > a > c
  | ⟨3, _⟩ => false  -- b > c > a: prefer c
  | ⟨4, _⟩ => false  -- c > a > b: prefer c
  | ⟨5, _⟩ => false  -- c > b > a: prefer c
end ArrowTheorem

namespace BooleanAnalysis
abbrev BoolCube (n : ℕ) := Fin n → Bool
end BooleanAnalysis

namespace BooleanAnalysis
variable {n : ℕ}
def boolToSign (b : Bool) : ℝ := if b then -1 else 1
end BooleanAnalysis

namespace ArrowTheorem
variable {n : ℕ}
def abVotes (p : Profile n) : BoolCube n := fun i => abPref (p i)
end ArrowTheorem

namespace ArrowTheorem
variable {n : ℕ}
def bcVotes (p : Profile n) : BoolCube n := fun i => bcPref (p i)
end ArrowTheorem

namespace ArrowTheorem
variable {n : ℕ}
def caVotes (p : Profile n) : BoolCube n := fun i => caPref (p i)
end ArrowTheorem

namespace BooleanAnalysis
abbrev BooleanFunc (n : ℕ) := BoolCube n → ℝ
end BooleanAnalysis

namespace ArrowTheorem
variable {n : ℕ}
def acyclic (f : BooleanFunc n) : Prop :=
  ∀ p : Profile n,
    ¬ (f (abVotes p) = 1 ∧ f (bcVotes p) = 1 ∧ f (caVotes p) = 1) ∧
    ¬ (f (abVotes p) = -1 ∧ f (bcVotes p) = -1 ∧ f (caVotes p) = -1)
end ArrowTheorem

namespace ArrowTheorem
variable {n : ℕ}
def unanimity (f : BooleanFunc n) : Prop :=
  f (fun _ => false) = 1
end ArrowTheorem

namespace BooleanAnalysis
variable {n : ℕ}
def isOddFunc (f : BooleanFunc n) : Prop :=
  ∀ x : BoolCube n, f (fun i => !x i) = -f x
end BooleanAnalysis

namespace BooleanAnalysis
variable {n : ℕ}
def isPmOne (f : BooleanFunc n) : Prop :=
  ∀ x : BoolCube n, f x = 1 ∨ f x = -1
end BooleanAnalysis

namespace BooleanAnalysis
variable {n : ℕ}
noncomputable def dictator (i : Fin n) : BooleanFunc n :=
  fun x ↦ boolToSign (x i)
end BooleanAnalysis

namespace ArrowTheorem
variable {n : ℕ}
def isDictator (f : BooleanFunc n) : Prop :=
  ∃ i : Fin n, f = dictator i
end ArrowTheorem

namespace ArrowTheorem
variable {n : ℕ}
theorem arrow_theorem (f : BooleanFunc n) (hodd : isOddFunc f) (hpm : isPmOne f)
    (huniv : unanimity f) (hacyc : acyclic f) :
    isDictator f := sorry
end ArrowTheorem
title: Arrow's Impossibility Theorem
statement_informal:
Arrow's Impossibility Theorem. Let $f:\{0,1\}^n\to\bbr$ be a social welfare function that is odd
(antisymmetric), $\pm 1$-valued, unanimous, and acyclic.  Then $f$ is a
\emph{dictatorship}: there exists a voter $i_0$ such that
$f = \mathrm{dict}_{i_0}$.

\emph{Proof:}
\begin{enumerate}
  \item Acyclicity $\Rightarrow$ $\mathrm{corr}(f) = -1/3$
        (Lemma~ArrowTheorem.acyclic_implies_corrFunc).
  \item $\mathrm{corr}(f) = -1/3$ $\Rightarrow$ $\hat f(S) = 0$ for $|S|\ne 1$
        (Lemma~ArrowTheorem.corrFunc_eq_neg_third_of_weight_one).
  \item Degree-1 + unanimous + $\pm 1$-valued $\Rightarrow$ dictator
        (Lemma~ArrowTheorem.degree_one_implies_dictator).
\end{enumerate}

definitions.title: Sign encoding
definitions.informal:
The \emph{sign encoding} maps $\mathrm{Bool}$ to $\{-1,1\}\subset\bbr$:
\[
  \sigma(b) \;=\; \begin{cases} 1 & b = \texttt{false},\\
                                -1 & b = \texttt{true}. \end{cases}
\]

definitions.title: Vote vectors
definitions.informal:
Given a profile $p$, the vote vectors $\mathrm{abVotes}(p)$,
$\mathrm{bcVotes}(p)$, $\mathrm{caVotes}(p) \in \{0,1\}^n$ record each
voter's pairwise preference.

definitions.title: Vote vectors
definitions.informal:
Given a profile $p$, the vote vectors $\mathrm{abVotes}(p)$,
$\mathrm{bcVotes}(p)$, $\mathrm{caVotes}(p) \in \{0,1\}^n$ record each
voter's pairwise preference.

definitions.title: Vote vectors
definitions.informal:
Given a profile $p$, the vote vectors $\mathrm{abVotes}(p)$,
$\mathrm{bcVotes}(p)$, $\mathrm{caVotes}(p) \in \{0,1\}^n$ record each
voter's pairwise preference.

definitions.title: Boolean function
definitions.informal: A \emph{Boolean function} of arity $n$ is a function $f : \{0,1\}^n \to \bbr$.

definitions.title: Acyclicity
definitions.informal:
A social welfare function $f$ is \emph{acyclic} if no profile of transitive
voter orderings produces a Condorcet cycle in society's preferences.
A cycle occurs when
$f(\mathrm{abVotes}(p)) = f(\mathrm{bcVotes}(p)) = f(\mathrm{caVotes}(p)) = 1$
(the cycle $a>b>c>a$) or all equal $-1$ (the reverse cycle).

definitions.title: Unanimity
definitions.informal:
A social welfare function $f:\{0,1\}^n\to\bbr$ is \emph{unanimous} if
$f(\texttt{false},\dots,\texttt{false}) = 1$: when all voters prefer
alternative $a$ over $b$, so does society.

definitions.title: Odd function
definitions.informal:
A Boolean function $f$ is \emph{odd} if $f(\lnot x) = -f(x)$ for all $x$.
This models antisymmetry of pairwise preferences in social choice.

definitions.title: $\pm 1$-valued function
definitions.informal: $f$ is \emph{$\pm 1$-valued} if $f(x)\in\{-1,1\}$ for all $x$.

definitions.title: Dictator
definitions.informal:
The \emph{dictator function} for coordinate $i$ outputs the sign of the $i$-th
bit: $\mathrm{dict}_i(x) = (-1)^{x_i}$.  Its unique nonzero Fourier coefficient
is $\widehat{\mathrm{dict}_i}(\{i\}) = 1$.

definitions.title: Dictatorship
definitions.informal:
$f$ is a \emph{dictatorship} if there exists a voter $i$ such that
$f = \mathrm{dict}_i$, i.e.\ society's preference always equals voter $i$'s.

informal_statement:
Definitions:
- Boolean hypercube. The \emph{Boolean hypercube} of dimension $n$ is $\{0,1\}^n$,
represented as the function type $\mathrm{Fin}\,n \to \mathrm{Bool}$.
- Sign encoding. The \emph{sign encoding} maps $\mathrm{Bool}$ to $\{-1,1\}\subset\bbr$:
\[
  \sigma(b) \;=\; \begin{cases} 1 & b = \texttt{false},\\
                                -1 & b = \texttt{true}. \end{cases}
\]
- Uniform weight. The uniform probability measure on $\{0,1\}^n$ assigns weight
$2^{-n}$ to each point.
- Boolean function. A \emph{Boolean function} of arity $n$ is a function $f : \{0,1\}^n \to \bbr$.
- Odd function. A Boolean function $f$ is \emph{odd} if $f(\lnot x) = -f(x)$ for all $x$.
This models antisymmetry of pairwise preferences in social choice.
- $\pm 1$-valued function. $f$ is \emph{$\pm 1$-valued} if $f(x)\in\{-1,1\}$ for all $x$.
- Walsh character. The \emph{Walsh--Fourier character} associated to $S\subseteq[n]$ is
\[
  \chi_S(x) \;=\; \prod_{i\in S}(-1)^{x_i}
  \;=\; \prod_{i\in S}\sigma(x_i).
\]
The family $\{\chi_S\}_{S\subseteq[n]}$ forms an orthonormal basis for
$L^2(\{0,1\}^n,\mathrm{uniform})$.
- Expectation. The \emph{expectation} of $f$ under the uniform measure:
\[
  \E[f] \;=\; 2^{-n}\sum_{x\in\{0,1\}^n} f(x).
\]
- $L^2$ inner product. The \emph{$L^2$ inner product} of two Boolean functions with respect to the
uniform measure:
\[
  \langle f, g\rangle \;=\; \E[f\cdot g]
  \;=\; 2^{-n}\sum_{x\in\{0,1\}^n} f(x)\,g(x).
\]
- Fourier coefficient. The \emph{Fourier--Walsh coefficient} of $f$ at frequency $S$ is
\[
  \hat f(S) \;=\; \langle f, \chi_S\rangle
  \;=\; 2^{-n}\sum_{x\in\{0,1\}^n} f(x)\,\chi_S(x).
\]
- Correlation function. The \emph{Fourier correlation function} of $f$ is
\[
  \mathrm{corr}(f) \;=\; \sum_{S\subseteq[n]} \hat f(S)^2\,(-1/3)^{|S|}.
\]
For odd $f$, only odd-level terms contribute.

Statement: Equality forces all weight on level 1. If $\mathrm{corr}(f) = -1/3$, then $\hat f(S) = 0$ for all $S$ with
$|S| \ne 1$.  Combined with oddness ($\hat f(\varnothing) = 0$), all Fourier
weight lies on level 1: $W_1[f] = 1$.
formal_statement:
import Mathlib

set_option maxHeartbeats 400000
open scoped BigOperators
scoped notation "⟪" f ", " g "⟫_𝔹" => innerProduct f g
scoped notation "χ_[" S "]" => chiS S
scoped notation f "̂(" S ")" => fourierCoeff f S
set_option maxHeartbeats 800000
open BooleanAnalysis

namespace BooleanAnalysis
abbrev BoolCube (n : ℕ) := Fin n → Bool
end BooleanAnalysis

namespace BooleanAnalysis
variable {n : ℕ}
def boolToSign (b : Bool) : ℝ := if b then -1 else 1
end BooleanAnalysis

namespace BooleanAnalysis
variable {n : ℕ}
noncomputable def uniformWeight (n : ℕ) : ℝ := (2 : ℝ)⁻¹ ^ n
end BooleanAnalysis

namespace BooleanAnalysis
abbrev BooleanFunc (n : ℕ) := BoolCube n → ℝ
end BooleanAnalysis

namespace BooleanAnalysis
variable {n : ℕ}
def isOddFunc (f : BooleanFunc n) : Prop :=
  ∀ x : BoolCube n, f (fun i => !x i) = -f x
end BooleanAnalysis

namespace BooleanAnalysis
variable {n : ℕ}
def isPmOne (f : BooleanFunc n) : Prop :=
  ∀ x : BoolCube n, f x = 1 ∨ f x = -1
end BooleanAnalysis

namespace BooleanAnalysis
variable {n : ℕ}
noncomputable def chiS (S : Finset (Fin n)) : BooleanFunc n :=
  fun x ↦ ∏ i ∈ S, boolToSign (x i)
end BooleanAnalysis

namespace BooleanAnalysis
variable {n : ℕ}
noncomputable def expect (f : BooleanFunc n) : ℝ :=
  uniformWeight n * ∑ x : BoolCube n, f x
end BooleanAnalysis

namespace BooleanAnalysis
variable {n : ℕ}
noncomputable def innerProduct (f g : BooleanFunc n) : ℝ :=
  expect (fun x ↦ f x * g x)
end BooleanAnalysis

namespace BooleanAnalysis
variable {n : ℕ}
noncomputable def fourierCoeff (f : BooleanFunc n) (S : Finset (Fin n)) : ℝ :=
  innerProduct f (chiS S)
end BooleanAnalysis

namespace ArrowTheorem
variable {n : ℕ}
noncomputable def corrFunc (f : BooleanFunc n) : ℝ :=
  ∑ S : Finset (Fin n), fourierCoeff f S ^ 2 * (-1/3 : ℝ) ^ S.card
end ArrowTheorem

namespace ArrowTheorem
variable {n : ℕ}
lemma corrFunc_eq_neg_third_of_weight_one {f : BooleanFunc n}
    (hodd : isOddFunc f) (hpm : isPmOne f) (hcorr : corrFunc f = -1/3) :
    ∀ S : Finset (Fin n), S.card ≠ 1 → fourierCoeff f S = 0 := sorry
end ArrowTheorem
title: Equality forces all weight on level 1
statement_informal:
Equality forces all weight on level 1. If $\mathrm{corr}(f) = -1/3$, then $\hat f(S) = 0$ for all $S$ with
$|S| \ne 1$.  Combined with oddness ($\hat f(\varnothing) = 0$), all Fourier
weight lies on level 1: $W_1[f] = 1$.

informal_statement:
Definitions:
- Boolean hypercube. The \emph{Boolean hypercube} of dimension $n$ is $\{0,1\}^n$,
represented as the function type $\mathrm{Fin}\,n \to \mathrm{Bool}$.
- Sign encoding. The \emph{sign encoding} maps $\mathrm{Bool}$ to $\{-1,1\}\subset\bbr$:
\[
  \sigma(b) \;=\; \begin{cases} 1 & b = \texttt{false},\\
                                -1 & b = \texttt{true}. \end{cases}
\]
- Uniform weight. The uniform probability measure on $\{0,1\}^n$ assigns weight
$2^{-n}$ to each point.
- Boolean function. A \emph{Boolean function} of arity $n$ is a function $f : \{0,1\}^n \to \bbr$.
- Odd function. A Boolean function $f$ is \emph{odd} if $f(\lnot x) = -f(x)$ for all $x$.
This models antisymmetry of pairwise preferences in social choice.
- $\pm 1$-valued function. $f$ is \emph{$\pm 1$-valued} if $f(x)\in\{-1,1\}$ for all $x$.
- Walsh character. The \emph{Walsh--Fourier character} associated to $S\subseteq[n]$ is
\[
  \chi_S(x) \;=\; \prod_{i\in S}(-1)^{x_i}
  \;=\; \prod_{i\in S}\sigma(x_i).
\]
The family $\{\chi_S\}_{S\subseteq[n]}$ forms an orthonormal basis for
$L^2(\{0,1\}^n,\mathrm{uniform})$.
- Expectation. The \emph{expectation} of $f$ under the uniform measure:
\[
  \E[f] \;=\; 2^{-n}\sum_{x\in\{0,1\}^n} f(x).
\]
- $L^2$ inner product. The \emph{$L^2$ inner product} of two Boolean functions with respect to the
uniform measure:
\[
  \langle f, g\rangle \;=\; \E[f\cdot g]
  \;=\; 2^{-n}\sum_{x\in\{0,1\}^n} f(x)\,g(x).
\]
- Fourier coefficient. The \emph{Fourier--Walsh coefficient} of $f$ at frequency $S$ is
\[
  \hat f(S) \;=\; \langle f, \chi_S\rangle
  \;=\; 2^{-n}\sum_{x\in\{0,1\}^n} f(x)\,\chi_S(x).
\]
- Correlation function. The \emph{Fourier correlation function} of $f$ is
\[
  \mathrm{corr}(f) \;=\; \sum_{S\subseteq[n]} \hat f(S)^2\,(-1/3)^{|S|}.
\]
For odd $f$, only odd-level terms contribute.

Statement: Correlation function lower bound. For any odd $\pm 1$-valued function,
\[
  \mathrm{corr}(f) \;\ge\; -1/3.
\]
\emph{Proof sketch:}  For odd $|S|\ge 1$ we have $(-1/3)^{|S|}\ge -1/3$.

informal_statement:
Definitions:
- Boolean hypercube. The \emph{Boolean hypercube} of dimension $n$ is $\{0,1\}^n$,
represented as the function type $\mathrm{Fin}\,n \to \mathrm{Bool}$.
- Sign encoding. The \emph{sign encoding} maps $\mathrm{Bool}$ to $\{-1,1\}\subset\bbr$:
\[
  \sigma(b) \;=\; \begin{cases} 1 & b = \texttt{false},\\
                                -1 & b = \texttt{true}. \end{cases}
\]
- Uniform weight. The uniform probability measure on $\{0,1\}^n$ assigns weight
$2^{-n}$ to each point.
- Boolean function. A \emph{Boolean function} of arity $n$ is a function $f : \{0,1\}^n \to \bbr$.
- Unanimity. A social welfare function $f:\{0,1\}^n\to\bbr$ is \emph{unanimous} if
$f(\texttt{false},\dots,\texttt{false}) = 1$: when all voters prefer
alternative $a$ over $b$, so does society.
- Odd function. A Boolean function $f$ is \emph{odd} if $f(\lnot x) = -f(x)$ for all $x$.
This models antisymmetry of pairwise preferences in social choice.
- $\pm 1$-valued function. $f$ is \emph{$\pm 1$-valued} if $f(x)\in\{-1,1\}$ for all $x$.
- Walsh character. The \emph{Walsh--Fourier character} associated to $S\subseteq[n]$ is
\[
  \chi_S(x) \;=\; \prod_{i\in S}(-1)^{x_i}
  \;=\; \prod_{i\in S}\sigma(x_i).
\]
The family $\{\chi_S\}_{S\subseteq[n]}$ forms an orthonormal basis for
$L^2(\{0,1\}^n,\mathrm{uniform})$.
- Dictator. The \emph{dictator function} for coordinate $i$ outputs the sign of the $i$-th
bit: $\mathrm{dict}_i(x) = (-1)^{x_i}$.  Its unique nonzero Fourier coefficient
is $\widehat{\mathrm{dict}_i}(\{i\}) = 1$.
- Expectation. The \emph{expectation} of $f$ under the uniform measure:
\[
  \E[f] \;=\; 2^{-n}\sum_{x\in\{0,1\}^n} f(x).
\]
- Dictatorship. $f$ is a \emph{dictatorship} if there exists a voter $i$ such that
$f = \mathrm{dict}_i$, i.e.\ society's preference always equals voter $i$'s.
- $L^2$ inner product. The \emph{$L^2$ inner product} of two Boolean functions with respect to the
uniform measure:
\[
  \langle f, g\rangle \;=\; \E[f\cdot g]
  \;=\; 2^{-n}\sum_{x\in\{0,1\}^n} f(x)\,g(x).
\]
- Fourier coefficient. The \emph{Fourier--Walsh coefficient} of $f$ at frequency $S$ is
\[
  \hat f(S) \;=\; \langle f, \chi_S\rangle
  \;=\; 2^{-n}\sum_{x\in\{0,1\}^n} f(x)\,\chi_S(x).
\]

Statement: Degree-1 unanimous $\pm 1$ function is a dictator. Suppose $f$ is $\pm 1$-valued, unanimous, and $\hat f(S) = 0$ for all $|S|\ne 1$.
Then $f$ is a dictator.

\emph{Proof sketch:}  Write $f = \sum_i a_i\chi_{\{i\}}$ where $a_i = \hat f(\{i\})$.
\begin{enumerate}
  \item Parseval: $\sum_i a_i^2 = 1$.
  \item Unanimity: $f(\mathbf{0}) = \sum_i a_i = 1$.
  \item For each $j$, the value $f(e_j) = 1 - 2a_j \in\{-1,1\}$ forces $a_j\in\{0,1\}$.
  \item From $a_j\in\{0,1\}$ and $\sum a_j^2 = \sum a_j = 1$: exactly one
        $a_{j_0} = 1$ and the rest are $0$.
  \item Hence $f = \chi_{\{j_0\}} = \mathrm{dict}_{j_0}$.
\end{enumerate}

open BooleanAnalysis

namespace BooleanAnalysis
abbrev BoolCube (n : ℕ) := Fin n → Bool
end BooleanAnalysis

namespace BooleanAnalysis
variable {n : ℕ}
def boolToSign (b : Bool) : ℝ := if b then -1 else 1
end BooleanAnalysis

namespace BooleanAnalysis
variable {n : ℕ}
noncomputable def uniformWeight (n : ℕ) : ℝ := (2 : ℝ)⁻¹ ^ n
end BooleanAnalysis

namespace BooleanAnalysis
abbrev BooleanFunc (n : ℕ) := BoolCube n → ℝ
end BooleanAnalysis

namespace ArrowTheorem
variable {n : ℕ}
def unanimity (f : BooleanFunc n) : Prop :=
  f (fun _ => false) = 1
end ArrowTheorem

namespace BooleanAnalysis
variable {n : ℕ}
def isOddFunc (f : BooleanFunc n) : Prop :=
  ∀ x : BoolCube n, f (fun i => !x i) = -f x
end BooleanAnalysis

namespace BooleanAnalysis
variable {n : ℕ}
def isPmOne (f : BooleanFunc n) : Prop :=
  ∀ x : BoolCube n, f x = 1 ∨ f x = -1
end BooleanAnalysis

namespace BooleanAnalysis
variable {n : ℕ}
noncomputable def chiS (S : Finset (Fin n)) : BooleanFunc n :=
  fun x ↦ ∏ i ∈ S, boolToSign (x i)
end BooleanAnalysis

namespace BooleanAnalysis
variable {n : ℕ}
noncomputable def dictator (i : Fin n) : BooleanFunc n :=
  fun x ↦ boolToSign (x i)
end BooleanAnalysis

namespace BooleanAnalysis
variable {n : ℕ}
noncomputable def expect (f : BooleanFunc n) : ℝ :=
  uniformWeight n * ∑ x : BoolCube n, f x
end BooleanAnalysis

namespace ArrowTheorem
variable {n : ℕ}
def isDictator (f : BooleanFunc n) : Prop :=
  ∃ i : Fin n, f = dictator i
end ArrowTheorem

namespace BooleanAnalysis
variable {n : ℕ}
noncomputable def innerProduct (f g : BooleanFunc n) : ℝ :=
  expect (fun x ↦ f x * g x)
end BooleanAnalysis

namespace BooleanAnalysis
variable {n : ℕ}
noncomputable def fourierCoeff (f : BooleanFunc n) (S : Finset (Fin n)) : ℝ :=
  innerProduct f (chiS S)
end BooleanAnalysis

namespace ArrowTheorem
variable {n : ℕ}
lemma degree_one_implies_dictator (f : BooleanFunc n) (_hodd : isOddFunc f)
    (hpm : isPmOne f) (huniv : unanimity f)
    (hdeg1 : ∀ S : Finset (Fin n), S.card ≠ 1 → fourierCoeff f S = 0) :
    isDictator f := sorry
end ArrowTheorem
title: Degree-1 unanimous $\pm 1$ function is a dictator
statement_informal:
Degree-1 unanimous $\pm 1$ function is a dictator. Suppose $f$ is $\pm 1$-valued, unanimous, and $\hat f(S) = 0$ for all $|S|\ne 1$.
Then $f$ is a dictator.

\emph{Proof sketch:}  Write $f = \sum_i a_i\chi_{\{i\}}$ where $a_i = \hat f(\{i\})$.
\begin{enumerate}
  \item Parseval: $\sum_i a_i^2 = 1$.
  \item Unanimity: $f(\mathbf{0}) = \sum_i a_i = 1$.
  \item For each $j$, the value $f(e_j) = 1 - 2a_j \in\{-1,1\}$ forces $a_j\in\{0,1\}$.
  \item From $a_j\in\{0,1\}$ and $\sum a_j^2 = \sum a_j = 1$: exactly one
        $a_{j_0} = 1$ and the rest are $0$.
  \item Hence $f = \chi_{\{j_0\}} = \mathrm{dict}_{j_0}$.
\end{enumerate}

definitions.title: Boolean hypercube
definitions.informal:
The \emph{Boolean hypercube} of dimension $n$ is $\{0,1\}^n$,
represented as the function type $\mathrm{Fin}\,n \to \mathrm{Bool}$.

definitions.title: Sign encoding
definitions.informal:
The \emph{sign encoding} maps $\mathrm{Bool}$ to $\{-1,1\}\subset\bbr$:
\[
  \sigma(b) \;=\; \begin{cases} 1 & b = \texttt{false},\\
                                -1 & b = \texttt{true}. \end{cases}
\]

definitions.title: Uniform weight
definitions.informal:
The uniform probability measure on $\{0,1\}^n$ assigns weight
$2^{-n}$ to each point.

definitions.title: Boolean function
definitions.informal: A \emph{Boolean function} of arity $n$ is a function $f : \{0,1\}^n \to \bbr$.

definitions.title: Unanimity
definitions.informal:
A social welfare function $f:\{0,1\}^n\to\bbr$ is \emph{unanimous} if
$f(\texttt{false},\dots,\texttt{false}) = 1$: when all voters prefer
alternative $a$ over $b$, so does society.

definitions.title: Odd function
definitions.informal:
A Boolean function $f$ is \emph{odd} if $f(\lnot x) = -f(x)$ for all $x$.
This models antisymmetry of pairwise preferences in social choice.

definitions.title: $\pm 1$-valued function
definitions.informal: $f$ is \emph{$\pm 1$-valued} if $f(x)\in\{-1,1\}$ for all $x$.

definitions.title: Walsh character
definitions.informal:
The \emph{Walsh--Fourier character} associated to $S\subseteq[n]$ is
\[
  \chi_S(x) \;=\; \prod_{i\in S}(-1)^{x_i}
  \;=\; \prod_{i\in S}\sigma(x_i).
\]
The family $\{\chi_S\}_{S\subseteq[n]}$ forms an orthonormal basis for
$L^2(\{0,1\}^n,\mathrm{uniform})$.

definitions.title: Dictator
definitions.informal:
The \emph{dictator function} for coordinate $i$ outputs the sign of the $i$-th
bit: $\mathrm{dict}_i(x) = (-1)^{x_i}$.  Its unique nonzero Fourier coefficient
is $\widehat{\mathrm{dict}_i}(\{i\}) = 1$.

definitions.title: Expectation
definitions.informal:
The \emph{expectation} of $f$ under the uniform measure:
\[
  \E[f] \;=\; 2^{-n}\sum_{x\in\{0,1\}^n} f(x).
\]

definitions.title: Dictatorship
definitions.informal:
$f$ is a \emph{dictatorship} if there exists a voter $i$ such that
$f = \mathrm{dict}_i$, i.e.\ society's preference always equals voter $i$'s.

definitions.title: $L^2$ inner product
definitions.informal:
The \emph{$L^2$ inner product} of two Boolean functions with respect to the
uniform measure:
\[
  \langle f, g\rangle \;=\; \E[f\cdot g]
  \;=\; 2^{-n}\sum_{x\in\{0,1\}^n} f(x)\,g(x).
\]

definitions.title: Fourier coefficient
definitions.informal:
The \emph{Fourier--Walsh coefficient} of $f$ at frequency $S$ is
\[
  \hat f(S) \;=\; \langle f, \chi_S\rangle
  \;=\; 2^{-n}\sum_{x\in\{0,1\}^n} f(x)\,\chi_S(x).
\]

informal_statement:
Definitions:
- Profile. A \emph{profile} assigns each of the $n$ voters one of the 6 orderings:
$p : \mathrm{Fin}\,n \to \mathrm{Fin}\,6$.
- Pairwise preference functions. $\mathrm{abPref}(k)$, $\mathrm{bcPref}(k)$, $\mathrm{caPref}(k)$ give the
Boolean preference of ordering $k\in\{0,\dots,5\}$ in the $a$-vs-$b$,
$b$-vs-$c$, and $c$-vs-$a$ comparisons, respectively.
- Pairwise preference functions. $\mathrm{abPref}(k)$, $\mathrm{bcPref}(k)$, $\mathrm{caPref}(k)$ give the
Boolean preference of ordering $k\in\{0,\dots,5\}$ in the $a$-vs-$b$,
$b$-vs-$c$, and $c$-vs-$a$ comparisons, respectively.
- Boolean hypercube. The \emph{Boolean hypercube} of dimension $n$ is $\{0,1\}^n$,
represented as the function type $\mathrm{Fin}\,n \to \mathrm{Bool}$.
- Sign encoding. The \emph{sign encoding} maps $\mathrm{Bool}$ to $\{-1,1\}\subset\bbr$:
\[
  \sigma(b) \;=\; \begin{cases} 1 & b = \texttt{false},\\
                                -1 & b = \texttt{true}. \end{cases}
\]
- Uniform weight. The uniform probability measure on $\{0,1\}^n$ assigns weight
$2^{-n}$ to each point.
- Vote vectors. Given a profile $p$, the vote vectors $\mathrm{abVotes}(p)$,
$\mathrm{bcVotes}(p)$, $\mathrm{caVotes}(p) \in \{0,1\}^n$ record each
voter's pairwise preference.
- Vote vectors. Given a profile $p$, the vote vectors $\mathrm{abVotes}(p)$,
$\mathrm{bcVotes}(p)$, $\mathrm{caVotes}(p) \in \{0,1\}^n$ record each
voter's pairwise preference.
- Boolean function. A \emph{Boolean function} of arity $n$ is a function $f : \{0,1\}^n \to \bbr$.
- Walsh character. The \emph{Walsh--Fourier character} associated to $S\subseteq[n]$ is
\[
  \chi_S(x) \;=\; \prod_{i\in S}(-1)^{x_i}
  \;=\; \prod_{i\in S}\sigma(x_i).
\]
The family $\{\chi_S\}_{S\subseteq[n]}$ forms an orthonormal basis for
$L^2(\{0,1\}^n,\mathrm{uniform})$.
- Expectation. The \emph{expectation} of $f$ under the uniform measure:
\[
  \E[f] \;=\; 2^{-n}\sum_{x\in\{0,1\}^n} f(x).
\]
- $L^2$ inner product. The \emph{$L^2$ inner product} of two Boolean functions with respect to the
uniform measure:
\[
  \langle f, g\rangle \;=\; \E[f\cdot g]
  \;=\; 2^{-n}\sum_{x\in\{0,1\}^n} f(x)\,g(x).
\]
- Fourier coefficient. The \emph{Fourier--Walsh coefficient} of $f$ at frequency $S$ is
\[
  \hat f(S) \;=\; \langle f, \chi_S\rangle
  \;=\; 2^{-n}\sum_{x\in\{0,1\}^n} f(x)\,\chi_S(x).
\]
- Correlation function. The \emph{Fourier correlation function} of $f$ is
\[
  \mathrm{corr}(f) \;=\; \sum_{S\subseteq[n]} \hat f(S)^2\,(-1/3)^{|S|}.
\]
For odd $f$, only odd-level terms contribute.

Statement: Expected product for the $ab$--$ca$ pair. For any $f:\{0,1\}^n\to\bbr$,
$\bigl(\tfrac16\bigr)^n\sum_p f(\mathrm{abVotes}(p))\,f(\mathrm{caVotes}(p))
 = \mathrm{corr}(f)$.
formal_statement:
import Mathlib

set_option maxHeartbeats 800000
open scoped BigOperators
open BooleanAnalysis
set_option maxHeartbeats 400000
scoped notation "⟪" f ", " g "⟫_𝔹" => innerProduct f g
scoped notation "χ_[" S "]" => chiS S
scoped notation f "̂(" S ")" => fourierCoeff f S

namespace ArrowTheorem
variable {n : ℕ}
abbrev Profile (n : ℕ) := Fin n → Fin 6
end ArrowTheorem

namespace ArrowTheorem
variable {n : ℕ}
def abPref : Fin 6 → Bool
  | ⟨0, _⟩ => false  -- a > b > c
  | ⟨1, _⟩ => false  -- a > c > b
  | ⟨2, _⟩ => true   -- b > a > c
  | ⟨3, _⟩ => true   -- b > c > a
  | ⟨4, _⟩ => false  -- c > a > b
  | ⟨5, _⟩ => true   -- c > b > a
end ArrowTheorem

namespace ArrowTheorem
variable {n : ℕ}
def caPref : Fin 6 → Bool
  | ⟨0, _⟩ => true   -- a > b > c: prefer a, so in ca: prefer a = true
  | ⟨1, _⟩ => true   -- a > c > b
  | ⟨2, _⟩ => true   -- b > a > c
  | ⟨3, _⟩ => false  -- b > c > a: prefer c
  | ⟨4, _⟩ => false  -- c > a > b: prefer c
  | ⟨5, _⟩ => false  -- c > b > a: prefer c
end ArrowTheorem

namespace BooleanAnalysis
abbrev BoolCube (n : ℕ) := Fin n → Bool
end BooleanAnalysis

namespace BooleanAnalysis
variable {n : ℕ}
def boolToSign (b : Bool) : ℝ := if b then -1 else 1
end BooleanAnalysis

namespace BooleanAnalysis
variable {n : ℕ}
noncomputable def uniformWeight (n : ℕ) : ℝ := (2 : ℝ)⁻¹ ^ n
end BooleanAnalysis

namespace ArrowTheorem
variable {n : ℕ}
def abVotes (p : Profile n) : BoolCube n := fun i => abPref (p i)
end ArrowTheorem

namespace ArrowTheorem
variable {n : ℕ}
def caVotes (p : Profile n) : BoolCube n := fun i => caPref (p i)
end ArrowTheorem

namespace BooleanAnalysis
abbrev BooleanFunc (n : ℕ) := BoolCube n → ℝ
end BooleanAnalysis

namespace BooleanAnalysis
variable {n : ℕ}
noncomputable def chiS (S : Finset (Fin n)) : BooleanFunc n :=
  fun x ↦ ∏ i ∈ S, boolToSign (x i)
end BooleanAnalysis

namespace BooleanAnalysis
variable {n : ℕ}
noncomputable def expect (f : BooleanFunc n) : ℝ :=
  uniformWeight n * ∑ x : BoolCube n, f x
end BooleanAnalysis

namespace BooleanAnalysis
variable {n : ℕ}
noncomputable def innerProduct (f g : BooleanFunc n) : ℝ :=
  expect (fun x ↦ f x * g x)
end BooleanAnalysis

namespace BooleanAnalysis
variable {n : ℕ}
noncomputable def fourierCoeff (f : BooleanFunc n) (S : Finset (Fin n)) : ℝ :=
  innerProduct f (chiS S)
end BooleanAnalysis

namespace ArrowTheorem
variable {n : ℕ}
noncomputable def corrFunc (f : BooleanFunc n) : ℝ :=
  ∑ S : Finset (Fin n), fourierCoeff f S ^ 2 * (-1/3 : ℝ) ^ S.card
end ArrowTheorem

namespace ArrowTheorem
variable {n : ℕ}
private lemma expected_product_abca (f : BooleanFunc n) :
    (1/6 : ℝ)^n * ∑ p : Profile n, f (abVotes p) * f (caVotes p) = corrFunc f := sorry
end ArrowTheorem
title: Expected product for the $ab$--$ca$ pair
statement_informal:
Expected product for the $ab$--$ca$ pair. For any $f:\{0,1\}^n\to\bbr$,
$\bigl(\tfrac16\bigr)^n\sum_p f(\mathrm{abVotes}(p))\,f(\mathrm{caVotes}(p))
 = \mathrm{corr}(f)$.

import Mathlib

set_option maxHeartbeats 800000
open scoped BigOperators
open BooleanAnalysis
set_option maxHeartbeats 400000
scoped notation "⟪" f ", " g "⟫_𝔹" => innerProduct f g
scoped notation "χ_[" S "]" => chiS S
scoped notation f "̂(" S ")" => fourierCoeff f S

namespace ArrowTheorem
variable {n : ℕ}
abbrev Profile (n : ℕ) := Fin n → Fin 6
end ArrowTheorem

namespace ArrowTheorem
variable {n : ℕ}
def bcPref : Fin 6 → Bool
  | ⟨0, _⟩ => false  -- a > b > c
  | ⟨1, _⟩ => true   -- a > c > b
  | ⟨2, _⟩ => false  -- b > a > c
  | ⟨3, _⟩ => false  -- b > c > a
  | ⟨4, _⟩ => true   -- c > a > b
  | ⟨5, _⟩ => true   -- c > b > a
end ArrowTheorem

namespace ArrowTheorem
variable {n : ℕ}
def caPref : Fin 6 → Bool
  | ⟨0, _⟩ => true   -- a > b > c: prefer a, so in ca: prefer a = true
  | ⟨1, _⟩ => true   -- a > c > b
  | ⟨2, _⟩ => true   -- b > a > c
  | ⟨3, _⟩ => false  -- b > c > a: prefer c
  | ⟨4, _⟩ => false  -- c > a > b: prefer c
  | ⟨5, _⟩ => false  -- c > b > a: prefer c
end ArrowTheorem

namespace BooleanAnalysis
abbrev BoolCube (n : ℕ) := Fin n → Bool
end BooleanAnalysis

namespace BooleanAnalysis
variable {n : ℕ}
def boolToSign (b : Bool) : ℝ := if b then -1 else 1
end BooleanAnalysis

namespace BooleanAnalysis
variable {n : ℕ}
noncomputable def uniformWeight (n : ℕ) : ℝ := (2 : ℝ)⁻¹ ^ n
end BooleanAnalysis

namespace ArrowTheorem
variable {n : ℕ}
def bcVotes (p : Profile n) : BoolCube n := fun i => bcPref (p i)
end ArrowTheorem

namespace ArrowTheorem
variable {n : ℕ}
def caVotes (p : Profile n) : BoolCube n := fun i => caPref (p i)
end ArrowTheorem

namespace BooleanAnalysis

end BooleanAnalysis

namespace BooleanAnalysis
variable {n : ℕ}
noncomputable def chiS (S : Finset (Fin n)) : BooleanFunc n :=
  fun x ↦ ∏ i ∈ S, boolToSign (x i)
end BooleanAnalysis

namespace BooleanAnalysis
variable {n : ℕ}
noncomputable def expect (f : BooleanFunc n) : ℝ :=
  uniformWeight n * ∑ x : BoolCube n, f x
end BooleanAnalysis

namespace BooleanAnalysis
variable {n : ℕ}
noncomputable def innerProduct (f g : BooleanFunc n) : ℝ :=
  expect (fun x ↦ f x * g x)
end BooleanAnalysis

namespace BooleanAnalysis
variable {n : ℕ}
noncomputable def fourierCoeff (f : BooleanFunc n) (S : Finset (Fin n)) : ℝ :=
  innerProduct f (chiS S)
end BooleanAnalysis

namespace ArrowTheorem
variable {n : ℕ}
noncomputable def corrFunc (f : BooleanFunc n) : ℝ :=
  ∑ S : Finset (Fin n), fourierCoeff f S ^ 2 * (-1/3 : ℝ) ^ S.card
end ArrowTheorem

variable {n : ℕ}

    (1/6 : ℝ)^n * ∑ p : Profile n, f (bcVotes p) * f (caVotes p) = corrFunc f := sorry
end ArrowTheorem

  \;=\; 2^{-n}\sum_{x\in\{0,1\}^n} f(x)\,g(x).
\]

definitions.title: Fourier coefficient
definitions.informal:
The \emph{Fourier--Walsh coefficient} of $f$ at frequency $S$ is
\[
  \hat f(S) \;=\; \langle f, \chi_S\rangle
  \;=\; 2^{-n}\sum_{x\in\{0,1\}^n} f(x)\,\chi_S(x).
\]

definitions.title: Correlation function
definitions.informal:
The \emph{Fourier correlation function} of $f$ is
\[
  \mathrm{corr}(f) \;=\; \sum_{S\subseteq[n]} \hat f(S)^2\,(-1/3)^{|S|}.
\]
For odd $f$, only odd-level terms contribute.

\[

\[

uniform measure:
\[

Statement: Expected pairwise product equals correlation function. For voters with i.i.d.\ uniform orderings,
\[
  \E_p\bigl[f(\mathrm{abVotes}(p))\cdot f(\mathrm{bcVotes}(p))\bigr]

import Mathlib

set_option maxHeartbeats 800000
open scoped BigOperators
open BooleanAnalysis
set_option maxHeartbeats 400000
scoped notation "⟪" f ", " g "⟫_𝔹" => innerProduct f g
scoped notation "χ_[" S "]" => chiS S
scoped notation f "̂(" S ")" => fourierCoeff f S

namespace ArrowTheorem
variable {n : ℕ}
abbrev Profile (n : ℕ) := Fin n → Fin 6
end ArrowTheorem

namespace ArrowTheorem
variable {n : ℕ}
def abPref : Fin 6 → Bool
  | ⟨0, _⟩ => false  -- a > b > c
  | ⟨1, _⟩ => false  -- a > c > b
  | ⟨2, _⟩ => true   -- b > a > c
  | ⟨3, _⟩ => true   -- b > c > a
  | ⟨4, _⟩ => false  -- c > a > b
  | ⟨5, _⟩ => true   -- c > b > a
end ArrowTheorem

namespace ArrowTheorem
variable {n : ℕ}
def bcPref : Fin 6 → Bool
  | ⟨0, _⟩ => false  -- a > b > c
  | ⟨1, _⟩ => true   -- a > c > b
  | ⟨2, _⟩ => false  -- b > a > c
  | ⟨3, _⟩ => false  -- b > c > a
  | ⟨4, _⟩ => true   -- c > a > b
  | ⟨5, _⟩ => true   -- c > b > a
end ArrowTheorem

namespace BooleanAnalysis
abbrev BoolCube (n : ℕ) := Fin n → Bool
end BooleanAnalysis

namespace BooleanAnalysis
variable {n : ℕ}
def boolToSign (b : Bool) : ℝ := if b then -1 else 1
end BooleanAnalysis

namespace BooleanAnalysis
variable {n : ℕ}
noncomputable def uniformWeight (n : ℕ) : ℝ := (2 : ℝ)⁻¹ ^ n
end BooleanAnalysis

namespace ArrowTheorem
variable {n : ℕ}
def abVotes (p : Profile n) : BoolCube n := fun i => abPref (p i)
end ArrowTheorem

namespace ArrowTheorem
variable {n : ℕ}
def bcVotes (p : Profile n) : BoolCube n := fun i => bcPref (p i)
end ArrowTheorem

namespace BooleanAnalysis
abbrev BooleanFunc (n : ℕ) := BoolCube n → ℝ
end BooleanAnalysis

namespace BooleanAnalysis
variable {n : ℕ}
noncomputable def chiS (S : Finset (Fin n)) : BooleanFunc n :=
  fun x ↦ ∏ i ∈ S, boolToSign (x i)
end BooleanAnalysis

namespace BooleanAnalysis
variable {n : ℕ}
noncomputable def expect (f : BooleanFunc n) : ℝ :=
  uniformWeight n * ∑ x : BoolCube n, f x
end BooleanAnalysis

namespace BooleanAnalysis
variable {n : ℕ}
noncomputable def innerProduct (f g : BooleanFunc n) : ℝ :=
  expect (fun x ↦ f x * g x)
end BooleanAnalysis

namespace BooleanAnalysis
variable {n : ℕ}
noncomputable def fourierCoeff (f : BooleanFunc n) (S : Finset (Fin n)) : ℝ :=
  innerProduct f (chiS S)
end BooleanAnalysis

namespace ArrowTheorem
variable {n : ℕ}
noncomputable def corrFunc (f : BooleanFunc n) : ℝ :=
  ∑ S : Finset (Fin n), fourierCoeff f S ^ 2 * (-1/3 : ℝ) ^ S.card
end ArrowTheorem

namespace ArrowTheorem
variable {n : ℕ}
lemma expected_product_eq_corrFunc (f : BooleanFunc n) :
    (1/6 : ℝ)^n * ∑ p : Profile n, f (abVotes p) * f (bcVotes p) = corrFunc f := sorry
end ArrowTheorem
title: Expected pairwise product equals correlation function
statement_informal:

\[
  \E_p\bigl[f(\mathrm{abVotes}(p))\cdot f(\mathrm{bcVotes}(p))\bigr]
  \;=\; \mathrm{corr}(f).
\]
The same identity holds for the $bc$--$ca$ and $ab$--$ca$ pairs.

Statement: Expected product from a kernel. Let $f:\{0,1\}^n\to\bbr$ and let $\mathrm{votes}_1,\mathrm{votes}_2$ map each
profile to a Boolean cube point.  If the pair satisfies the kernel identity
$\bigl(\tfrac16\bigr)^n\sum_p \chi_S(\mathrm{votes}_1(p))\,\chi_T(\mathrm{votes}_2(p))

abbrev Profile (n : ℕ) := Fin n → Fin 6

namespace BooleanAnalysis
variable {n : ℕ}
def boolToSign (b : Bool) : ℝ := if b then -1 else 1

namespace BooleanAnalysis

namespace BooleanAnalysis
abbrev BooleanFunc (n : ℕ) := BoolCube n → ℝ
end BooleanAnalysis

namespace BooleanAnalysis
variable {n : ℕ}
noncomputable def chiS (S : Finset (Fin n)) : BooleanFunc n :=
  fun x ↦ ∏ i ∈ S, boolToSign (x i)
end BooleanAnalysis

namespace BooleanAnalysis
variable {n : ℕ}
noncomputable def expect (f : BooleanFunc n) : ℝ :=
  uniformWeight n * ∑ x : BoolCube n, f x
end BooleanAnalysis

  ∑ S : Finset (Fin n), fourierCoeff f S ^ 2 * (-1/3 : ℝ) ^ S.card
end ArrowTheorem

namespace ArrowTheorem
variable {n : ℕ}
private lemma expected_product_helper (f : BooleanFunc n)
    (votes1 votes2 : Profile n → BoolCube n)
    (hkernel : ∀ S T : Finset (Fin n),
      (1/6 : ℝ)^n * ∑ p : Profile n, chiS S (votes1 p) * chiS T (votes2 p) =
      if S = T then (-1/3 : ℝ)^S.card else 0) :
    (1/6 : ℝ)^n * ∑ p : Profile n, f (votes1 p) * f (votes2 p) = corrFunc f := sorry
end ArrowTheorem
title: Expected product from a kernel
statement_informal:
Expected product from a kernel. Let $f:\{0,1\}^n\to\bbr$ and let $\mathrm{votes}_1,\mathrm{votes}_2$ map each
profile to a Boolean cube point.  If the pair satisfies the kernel identity
$\bigl(\tfrac16\bigr)^n\sum_p \chi_S(\mathrm{votes}_1(p))\,\chi_T(\mathrm{votes}_2(p))
 = [S = T]\,(-1/3)^{|S|}$ for all $S,T$, then
\[
  \Bigl(\tfrac16\Bigr)^{\!n}\sum_{p} f(\mathrm{votes}_1(p))\,f(\mathrm{votes}_2(p))
  \;=\; \mathrm{corr}(f).
\]

informal_statement:
Product over a finset as an indicator product. For $A\subseteq\mathrm{Fin}\,n$ and $g:\mathrm{Fin}\,n\to\bbr$,
\[
  \prod_{j\in A} g(j) \;=\; \prod_{j:\mathrm{Fin}\,n}
    \begin{cases} g(j) & j\in A \\ 1 & j\notin A \end{cases},
\]
rewriting a product over a finset as a product over all of $\mathrm{Fin}\,n$
with an indicator.

import Mathlib

set_option maxHeartbeats 800000
open scoped BigOperators

namespace ArrowTheorem
variable {n : ℕ}
private lemma prod_finset_eq_prod_univ_ite {n : ℕ} (A : Finset (Fin n)) (g : Fin n → ℝ) :
    ∏ j ∈ A, g j = ∏ j : Fin n, if j ∈ A then g j else 1 := sorry
end ArrowTheorem
title: Product over a finset as an indicator product
statement_informal:
Product over a finset as an indicator product. For $A\subseteq\mathrm{Fin}\,n$ and $g:\mathrm{Fin}\,n\to\bbr$,
\[
  \prod_{j\in A} g(j) \;=\; \prod_{j:\mathrm{Fin}\,n}
    \begin{cases} g(j) & j\in A \\ 1 & j\notin A \end{cases},
\]
rewriting a product over a finset as a product over all of $\mathrm{Fin}\,n$
with an indicator.

\[

                                -1 & b = \texttt{true}. \end{cases}
\]

$\mathrm{bcVotes}(p)$, $\mathrm{caVotes}(p) \in \{0,1\}^n$ record each

- Vote vectors. Given a profile $p$, the vote vectors $\mathrm{abVotes}(p)$,

Statement: $ab$--$bc$ kernel. Specialization of the general kernel to the $ab$--$bc$ vote pair: for all

namespace BooleanAnalysis
variable {n : ℕ}
def boolToSign (b : Bool) : ℝ := if b then -1 else 1
end BooleanAnalysis

namespace ArrowTheorem
variable {n : ℕ}
def abVotes (p : Profile n) : BoolCube n := fun i => abPref (p i)
end ArrowTheorem

namespace ArrowTheorem
variable {n : ℕ}
def bcVotes (p : Profile n) : BoolCube n := fun i => bcPref (p i)
end ArrowTheorem

namespace BooleanAnalysis
abbrev BooleanFunc (n : ℕ) := BoolCube n → ℝ
end BooleanAnalysis

namespace BooleanAnalysis
variable {n : ℕ}
noncomputable def chiS (S : Finset (Fin n)) : BooleanFunc n :=
  fun x ↦ ∏ i ∈ S, boolToSign (x i)
end BooleanAnalysis

namespace ArrowTheorem
variable {n : ℕ}
private lemma profile_inner_product_kernel (S T : Finset (Fin n)) :
    (1/6 : ℝ)^n * ∑ p : Profile n,
      chiS S (abVotes p) * chiS T (bcVotes p) =
    if S = T then (-1/3 : ℝ)^S.card else 0 := sorry
end ArrowTheorem
title: $ab$--$bc$ kernel

definitions.title: Profile
definitions.informal:
A \emph{profile} assigns each of the $n$ voters one of the 6 orderings:
$p : \mathrm{Fin}\,n \to \mathrm{Fin}\,6$.

definitions.title: Pairwise preference functions
definitions.informal:
$\mathrm{abPref}(k)$, $\mathrm{bcPref}(k)$, $\mathrm{caPref}(k)$ give the
Boolean preference of ordering $k\in\{0,\dots,5\}$ in the $a$-vs-$b$,
$b$-vs-$c$, and $c$-vs-$a$ comparisons, respectively.

definitions.title: Pairwise preference functions
definitions.informal:
$\mathrm{abPref}(k)$, $\mathrm{bcPref}(k)$, $\mathrm{caPref}(k)$ give the
Boolean preference of ordering $k\in\{0,\dots,5\}$ in the $a$-vs-$b$,
$b$-vs-$c$, and $c$-vs-$a$ comparisons, respectively.

definitions.title: Boolean hypercube
definitions.informal:
The \emph{Boolean hypercube} of dimension $n$ is $\{0,1\}^n$,
represented as the function type $\mathrm{Fin}\,n \to \mathrm{Bool}$.

definitions.title: Sign encoding
definitions.informal:
The \emph{sign encoding} maps $\mathrm{Bool}$ to $\{-1,1\}\subset\bbr$:
\[
  \sigma(b) \;=\; \begin{cases} 1 & b = \texttt{false},\\
                                -1 & b = \texttt{true}. \end{cases}
\]

definitions.title: Vote vectors
definitions.informal:
Given a profile $p$, the vote vectors $\mathrm{abVotes}(p)$,
$\mathrm{bcVotes}(p)$, $\mathrm{caVotes}(p) \in \{0,1\}^n$ record each
voter's pairwise preference.

definitions.title: Vote vectors
definitions.informal:
Given a profile $p$, the vote vectors $\mathrm{abVotes}(p)$,
$\mathrm{bcVotes}(p)$, $\mathrm{caVotes}(p) \in \{0,1\}^n$ record each
voter's pairwise preference.

definitions.title: Boolean function
definitions.informal: A \emph{Boolean function} of arity $n$ is a function $f : \{0,1\}^n \to \bbr$.

definitions.title: Walsh character
definitions.informal:
The \emph{Walsh--Fourier character} associated to $S\subseteq[n]$ is
\[
  \chi_S(x) \;=\; \prod_{i\in S}(-1)^{x_i}
  \;=\; \prod_{i\in S}\sigma(x_i).
\]
The family $\{\chi_S\}_{S\subseteq[n]}$ forms an orthonormal basis for
$L^2(\{0,1\}^n,\mathrm{uniform})$.

informal_statement:
Definitions:
- Profile. A \emph{profile} assigns each of the $n$ voters one of the 6 orderings:
$p : \mathrm{Fin}\,n \to \mathrm{Fin}\,6$.
- Pairwise preference functions. $\mathrm{abPref}(k)$, $\mathrm{bcPref}(k)$, $\mathrm{caPref}(k)$ give the
Boolean preference of ordering $k\in\{0,\dots,5\}$ in the $a$-vs-$b$,
$b$-vs-$c$, and $c$-vs-$a$ comparisons, respectively.
- Pairwise preference functions. $\mathrm{abPref}(k)$, $\mathrm{bcPref}(k)$, $\mathrm{caPref}(k)$ give the
Boolean preference of ordering $k\in\{0,\dots,5\}$ in the $a$-vs-$b$,
$b$-vs-$c$, and $c$-vs-$a$ comparisons, respectively.
- Boolean hypercube. The \emph{Boolean hypercube} of dimension $n$ is $\{0,1\}^n$,
represented as the function type $\mathrm{Fin}\,n \to \mathrm{Bool}$.
- Sign encoding. The \emph{sign encoding} maps $\mathrm{Bool}$ to $\{-1,1\}\subset\bbr$:
\[
  \sigma(b) \;=\; \begin{cases} 1 & b = \texttt{false},\\
                                -1 & b = \texttt{true}. \end{cases}
\]
- Vote vectors. Given a profile $p$, the vote vectors $\mathrm{abVotes}(p)$,
$\mathrm{bcVotes}(p)$, $\mathrm{caVotes}(p) \in \{0,1\}^n$ record each
voter's pairwise preference.
- Vote vectors. Given a profile $p$, the vote vectors $\mathrm{abVotes}(p)$,
$\mathrm{bcVotes}(p)$, $\mathrm{caVotes}(p) \in \{0,1\}^n$ record each
voter's pairwise preference.
- Boolean function. A \emph{Boolean function} of arity $n$ is a function $f : \{0,1\}^n \to \bbr$.
- Walsh character. The \emph{Walsh--Fourier character} associated to $S\subseteq[n]$ is
\[
  \chi_S(x) \;=\; \prod_{i\in S}(-1)^{x_i}
  \;=\; \prod_{i\in S}\sigma(x_i).
\]
The family $\{\chi_S\}_{S\subseteq[n]}$ forms an orthonormal basis for
$L^2(\{0,1\}^n,\mathrm{uniform})$.

Statement: $ab$--$ca$ kernel. Specialization of the general kernel to the $ab$--$ca$ vote pair: for all
$S,T\subseteq[n]$,
\[
  \Bigl(\tfrac16\Bigr)^{\!n}\sum_{p}
    \chi_S(\mathrm{abVotes}(p))\,\chi_T(\mathrm{caVotes}(p))
  \;=\;
  \begin{cases} (-1/3)^{|S|} & S = T \\ 0 & S\ne T. \end{cases}
\]

import Mathlib

set_option maxHeartbeats 800000
open scoped BigOperators
open BooleanAnalysis
set_option maxHeartbeats 400000
scoped notation "⟪" f ", " g "⟫_𝔹" => innerProduct f g
scoped notation "χ_[" S "]" => chiS S
scoped notation f "̂(" S ")" => fourierCoeff f S

namespace ArrowTheorem
variable {n : ℕ}
abbrev Profile (n : ℕ) := Fin n → Fin 6
end ArrowTheorem

namespace ArrowTheorem
variable {n : ℕ}
def abPref : Fin 6 → Bool
  | ⟨0, _⟩ => false  -- a > b > c
  | ⟨1, _⟩ => false  -- a > c > b
  | ⟨2, _⟩ => true   -- b > a > c
  | ⟨3, _⟩ => true   -- b > c > a
  | ⟨4, _⟩ => false  -- c > a > b
  | ⟨5, _⟩ => true   -- c > b > a
end ArrowTheorem

namespace ArrowTheorem
variable {n : ℕ}
def caPref : Fin 6 → Bool
  | ⟨0, _⟩ => true   -- a > b > c: prefer a, so in ca: prefer a = true
  | ⟨1, _⟩ => true   -- a > c > b
  | ⟨2, _⟩ => true   -- b > a > c
  | ⟨3, _⟩ => false  -- b > c > a: prefer c
  | ⟨4, _⟩ => false  -- c > a > b: prefer c
  | ⟨5, _⟩ => false  -- c > b > a: prefer c
end ArrowTheorem

namespace BooleanAnalysis
abbrev BoolCube (n : ℕ) := Fin n → Bool
end BooleanAnalysis

namespace BooleanAnalysis
variable {n : ℕ}
def boolToSign (b : Bool) : ℝ := if b then -1 else 1
end BooleanAnalysis

namespace ArrowTheorem
variable {n : ℕ}
def abVotes (p : Profile n) : BoolCube n := fun i => abPref (p i)
end ArrowTheorem

namespace ArrowTheorem
variable {n : ℕ}
def caVotes (p : Profile n) : BoolCube n := fun i => caPref (p i)
end ArrowTheorem

namespace BooleanAnalysis
abbrev BooleanFunc (n : ℕ) := BoolCube n → ℝ
end BooleanAnalysis

namespace BooleanAnalysis
variable {n : ℕ}
noncomputable def chiS (S : Finset (Fin n)) : BooleanFunc n :=
  fun x ↦ ∏ i ∈ S, boolToSign (x i)
end BooleanAnalysis

namespace ArrowTheorem
variable {n : ℕ}
private lemma profile_kernel_abca (S T : Finset (Fin n)) :
    (1/6 : ℝ)^n * ∑ p : Profile n,
      chiS S (abVotes p) * chiS T (caVotes p) =
    if S = T then (-1/3 : ℝ)^S.card else 0 := sorry
end ArrowTheorem

informal_statement:
Definitions:
- Profile. A \emph{profile} assigns each of the $n$ voters one of the 6 orderings:
$p : \mathrm{Fin}\,n \to \mathrm{Fin}\,6$.
- Boolean hypercube. The \emph{Boolean hypercube} of dimension $n$ is $\{0,1\}^n$,
represented as the function type $\mathrm{Fin}\,n \to \mathrm{Bool}$.
- Sign encoding. The \emph{sign encoding} maps $\mathrm{Bool}$ to $\{-1,1\}\subset\bbr$:
\[
  \sigma(b) \;=\; \begin{cases} 1 & b = \texttt{false},\\
                                -1 & b = \texttt{true}. \end{cases}
\]
- Boolean function. A \emph{Boolean function} of arity $n$ is a function $f : \{0,1\}^n \to \bbr$.
- Walsh character. The \emph{Walsh--Fourier character} associated to $S\subseteq[n]$ is
\[
  \chi_S(x) \;=\; \prod_{i\in S}(-1)^{x_i}
  \;=\; \prod_{i\in S}\sigma(x_i).
\]
The family $\{\chi_S\}_{S\subseteq[n]}$ forms an orthonormal basis for
$L^2(\{0,1\}^n,\mathrm{uniform})$.

Statement: General profile kernel. Let $\mathrm{xPref},\mathrm{yPref}:\mathrm{Fin}\,6\to\mathrm{Bool}$ be two
preference assignments with balanced marginals
$\sum_k s(\mathrm{xPref}(k)) = \sum_k s(\mathrm{yPref}(k)) = 0$ and cross-sum
$\sum_k s(\mathrm{xPref}(k))\,s(\mathrm{yPref}(k)) = -2$.  Then for all
$S,T\subseteq[n]$,
\[
  \Bigl(\tfrac16\Bigr)^{\!n}\sum_{p:\mathrm{Profile}\,n}
    \chi_S\bigl(\mathrm{xPref}\circ p\bigr)\,\chi_T\bigl(\mathrm{yPref}\circ p\bigr)
  \;=\;
  \begin{cases} (-1/3)^{|S|} & S = T \\ 0 & S\ne T. \end{cases}
\]

set_option maxHeartbeats 800000
open scoped BigOperators
open BooleanAnalysis
set_option maxHeartbeats 400000
scoped notation "⟪" f ", " g "⟫_𝔹" => innerProduct f g
scoped notation "χ_[" S "]" => chiS S
scoped notation f "̂(" S ")" => fourierCoeff f S

namespace ArrowTheorem
variable {n : ℕ}
abbrev Profile (n : ℕ) := Fin n → Fin 6
end ArrowTheorem

namespace BooleanAnalysis
abbrev BoolCube (n : ℕ) := Fin n → Bool
end BooleanAnalysis

namespace BooleanAnalysis
variable {n : ℕ}
def boolToSign (b : Bool) : ℝ := if b then -1 else 1
end BooleanAnalysis

namespace BooleanAnalysis
abbrev BooleanFunc (n : ℕ) := BoolCube n → ℝ
end BooleanAnalysis

namespace BooleanAnalysis
variable {n : ℕ}
noncomputable def chiS (S : Finset (Fin n)) : BooleanFunc n :=
  fun x ↦ ∏ i ∈ S, boolToSign (x i)
end BooleanAnalysis

namespace ArrowTheorem
variable {n : ℕ}
private lemma profile_kernel_gen {xPref yPref : Fin 6 → Bool}
    (hx : ∑ k : Fin 6, boolToSign (xPref k) = 0)
    (hy : ∑ k : Fin 6, boolToSign (yPref k) = 0)
    (hxy : ∑ k : Fin 6, boolToSign (xPref k) * boolToSign (yPref k) = -2)
    (S T : Finset (Fin n)) :
    (1/6 : ℝ)^n * ∑ p : Profile n,
      chiS S (fun i => xPref (p i)) * chiS T (fun i => yPref (p i)) =
    if S = T then (-1/3 : ℝ)^S.card else 0 := sorry
end ArrowTheorem
title: General profile kernel
statement_informal:
General profile kernel. Let $\mathrm{xPref},\mathrm{yPref}:\mathrm{Fin}\,6\to\mathrm{Bool}$ be two
preference assignments with balanced marginals
$\sum_k s(\mathrm{xPref}(k)) = \sum_k s(\mathrm{yPref}(k)) = 0$ and cross-sum
$\sum_k s(\mathrm{xPref}(k))\,s(\mathrm{yPref}(k)) = -2$.  Then for all
$S,T\subseteq[n]$,
\[
  \Bigl(\tfrac16\Bigr)^{\!n}\sum_{p:\mathrm{Profile}\,n}
    \chi_S\bigl(\mathrm{xPref}\circ p\bigr)\,\chi_T\bigl(\mathrm{yPref}\circ p\bigr)
  \;=\;
  \begin{cases} (-1/3)^{|S|} & S = T \\ 0 & S\ne T. \end{cases}
\]

definitions.title: Profile
definitions.informal:
A \emph{profile} assigns each of the $n$ voters one of the 6 orderings:
$p : \mathrm{Fin}\,n \to \mathrm{Fin}\,6$.

definitions.title: Boolean hypercube
definitions.informal:
The \emph{Boolean hypercube} of dimension $n$ is $\{0,1\}^n$,
represented as the function type $\mathrm{Fin}\,n \to \mathrm{Bool}$.

definitions.title: Sign encoding
definitions.informal:
The \emph{sign encoding} maps $\mathrm{Bool}$ to $\{-1,1\}\subset\bbr$:
\[
  \sigma(b) \;=\; \begin{cases} 1 & b = \texttt{false},\\
                                -1 & b = \texttt{true}. \end{cases}
\]

definitions.title: Boolean function
definitions.informal: A \emph{Boolean function} of arity $n$ is a function $f : \{0,1\}^n \to \bbr$.

definitions.title: Walsh character
definitions.informal:
The \emph{Walsh--Fourier character} associated to $S\subseteq[n]$ is
\[
  \chi_S(x) \;=\; \prod_{i\in S}(-1)^{x_i}
  \;=\; \prod_{i\in S}\sigma(x_i).
\]
The family $\{\chi_S\}_{S\subseteq[n]}$ forms an orthonormal basis for
$L^2(\{0,1\}^n,\mathrm{uniform})$.

informal_statement:
Definitions:
- Pairwise preference functions. $\mathrm{abPref}(k)$, $\mathrm{bcPref}(k)$, $\mathrm{caPref}(k)$ give the
Boolean preference of ordering $k\in\{0,\dots,5\}$ in the $a$-vs-$b$,
$b$-vs-$c$, and $c$-vs-$a$ comparisons, respectively.
- Pairwise preference functions. $\mathrm{abPref}(k)$, $\mathrm{bcPref}(k)$, $\mathrm{caPref}(k)$ give the
Boolean preference of ordering $k\in\{0,\dots,5\}$ in the $a$-vs-$b$,
$b$-vs-$c$, and $c$-vs-$a$ comparisons, respectively.
- Pairwise preference functions. $\mathrm{abPref}(k)$, $\mathrm{bcPref}(k)$, $\mathrm{caPref}(k)$ give the
Boolean preference of ordering $k\in\{0,\dots,5\}$ in the $a$-vs-$b$,
$b$-vs-$c$, and $c$-vs-$a$ comparisons, respectively.
- Sign encoding. The \emph{sign encoding} maps $\mathrm{Bool}$ to $\{-1,1\}\subset\bbr$:
\[
  \sigma(b) \;=\; \begin{cases} 1 & b = \texttt{false},\\
                                -1 & b = \texttt{true}. \end{cases}
\]

Statement: Pairwise sign sums. Summing pairwise sign products over all 6 orderings:
\[
  \sum_{k=0}^{5} s_{ab}(k)\cdot s_{bc}(k)
  \;=\; \sum_{k=0}^{5} s_{bc}(k)\cdot s_{ca}(k)
  \;=\; \sum_{k=0}^{5} s_{ab}(k)\cdot s_{ca}(k)
  \;=\; -2.
\]
Hence $\E[s_{ab}\cdot s_{bc}] = -2/6 = -1/3$.
formal_statement:
import Mathlib

set_option maxHeartbeats 800000
open scoped BigOperators
open BooleanAnalysis

namespace ArrowTheorem
variable {n : ℕ}
def abPref : Fin 6 → Bool
  | ⟨0, _⟩ => false  -- a > b > c
  | ⟨1, _⟩ => false  -- a > c > b
  | ⟨2, _⟩ => true   -- b > a > c
  | ⟨3, _⟩ => true   -- b > c > a
  | ⟨4, _⟩ => false  -- c > a > b
  | ⟨5, _⟩ => true   -- c > b > a
end ArrowTheorem

namespace ArrowTheorem
variable {n : ℕ}
def bcPref : Fin 6 → Bool
  | ⟨0, _⟩ => false  -- a > b > c
  | ⟨1, _⟩ => true   -- a > c > b
  | ⟨2, _⟩ => false  -- b > a > c
  | ⟨3, _⟩ => false  -- b > c > a
  | ⟨4, _⟩ => true   -- c > a > b
  | ⟨5, _⟩ => true   -- c > b > a
end ArrowTheorem

namespace BooleanAnalysis
variable {n : ℕ}
def boolToSign (b : Bool) : ℝ := if b then -1 else 1
end BooleanAnalysis

namespace ArrowTheorem
variable {n : ℕ}
lemma sum_abPref_bcPref :
    ∑ k : Fin 6, boolToSign (abPref k) * boolToSign (bcPref k) = -2 := sorry
end ArrowTheorem
title: Pairwise sign sums
statement_informal:
Pairwise sign sums. Summing pairwise sign products over all 6 orderings:
\[
  \sum_{k=0}^{5} s_{ab}(k)\cdot s_{bc}(k)
  \;=\; \sum_{k=0}^{5} s_{bc}(k)\cdot s_{ca}(k)
  \;=\; \sum_{k=0}^{5} s_{ab}(k)\cdot s_{ca}(k)
  \;=\; -2.
\]
Hence $\E[s_{ab}\cdot s_{bc}] = -2/6 = -1/3$.

definitions.title: Pairwise preference functions
definitions.informal:
$\mathrm{abPref}(k)$, $\mathrm{bcPref}(k)$, $\mathrm{caPref}(k)$ give the
Boolean preference of ordering $k\in\{0,\dots,5\}$ in the $a$-vs-$b$,
$b$-vs-$c$, and $c$-vs-$a$ comparisons, respectively.

definitions.title: Pairwise preference functions
definitions.informal:
$\mathrm{abPref}(k)$, $\mathrm{bcPref}(k)$, $\mathrm{caPref}(k)$ give the
Boolean preference of ordering $k\in\{0,\dots,5\}$ in the $a$-vs-$b$,

$b$-vs-$c$, and $c$-vs-$a$ comparisons, respectively.

definitions.title: Sign encoding
definitions.informal:
The \emph{sign encoding} maps $\mathrm{Bool}$ to $\{-1,1\}\subset\bbr$:
\[
  \sigma(b) \;=\; \begin{cases} 1 & b = \texttt{false},\\
                                -1 & b = \texttt{true}. \end{cases}
\]

Boolean preference of ordering $k\in\{0,\dots,5\}$ in the $a$-vs-$b$,
$b$-vs-$c$, and $c$-vs-$a$ comparisons, respectively.

def caPref : Fin 6 → Bool
  | ⟨0, _⟩ => true   -- a > b > c: prefer a, so in ca: prefer a = true
  | ⟨1, _⟩ => true   -- a > c > b
  | ⟨2, _⟩ => true   -- b > a > c
  | ⟨3, _⟩ => false  -- b > c > a: prefer c
  | ⟨4, _⟩ => false  -- c > a > b: prefer c
  | ⟨5, _⟩ => false  -- c > b > a: prefer c
end ArrowTheorem

namespace BooleanAnalysis
variable {n : ℕ}

title: Sum of $ca$ preference signs vanishes

definitions.title: Pairwise preference functions
definitions.informal:
$\mathrm{abPref}(k)$, $\mathrm{bcPref}(k)$, $\mathrm{caPref}(k)$ give the
Boolean preference of ordering $k\in\{0,\dots,5\}$ in the $a$-vs-$b$,
$b$-vs-$c$, and $c$-vs-$a$ comparisons, respectively.

definitions.title: Sign encoding
definitions.informal:
The \emph{sign encoding} maps $\mathrm{Bool}$ to $\{-1,1\}\subset\bbr$:
\[
  \sigma(b) \;=\; \begin{cases} 1 & b = \texttt{false},\\
                                -1 & b = \texttt{true}. \end{cases}
\]

informal_statement:
Definitions:
- Binomial probability mass function. The binomial probability mass function, $\Pr[\mathrm{Bin}(n,p) = k] = \binom{n}{k}\, p^k\, (1-p)^{n-k}$.
- Restriction. A restriction on $n$ variables is a map $\rho : \mathrm{Fin}\,n \to \mathrm{Option}\,\mathrm{Bool}$,
assigning each variable either a fixed Boolean value or leaving it free (none).
- Free variables of a restriction. The set of free variables of a restriction $\rho$ is the finite set of indices
$i \in \mathrm{Fin}\,n$ for which $\rho(i)$ is unset (none).
- Restrictions with a fixed number of free variables. For natural numbers $n$ and $k$, this is the finite set of restrictions on $n$ variables
whose set of free variables has cardinality exactly $k$.
- Bernoulli weight of a restriction. For a parameter $p \in \bbr$ and a restriction $\rho$ on $n$ variables, the Bernoulli
weight is
\[
  p^{\abs{\mathrm{freeVars}(\rho)}} \cdot \left(\tfrac{1-p}{2}\right)^{\,n - \abs{\mathrm{freeVars}(\rho)}},
\]
so each free variable contributes a factor $p$ and each fixed variable a factor
$(1-p)/2$.
- Fixed-size restriction probability $R_k$. The probability of an event under the fixed-size restriction model $R_k$, which is uniform
over restrictions with exactly $k$ free variables. It is defined as the fraction of
restrictions with exactly $k$ free variables that satisfy the event.
- Bernoulli event probability. For a parameter $p$ and a (decidable) predicate $\mathrm{event}$ on restrictions, the
probability that the predicate holds under a Bernoulli($p$) random restriction is the
sum over all restrictions $\rho$ of $\mathrm{bernoulliRestrWeight}(p,\rho)$ weighted by
the indicator of $\mathrm{event}(\rho)$.

Statement: Decomposition of Bernoulli probability by free-variable count. For $0 \le p \le 1$ and any event, the Bernoulli$(p)$ restriction probability decomposes
according to the number of free variables:
\[
  \Pr_{R_p}[\mathrm{event}] = \sum_{k=0}^{n} \mathrm{binomialPMF}\,n\,p\,k \cdot \mathrm{fixedSizeRestrProb}\,(\mathrm{event})\,k,
\]
because conditioning $R_p$ on having exactly $k$ free variables yields exactly the model $R_k$.
formal_statement:
import Mathlib

open SwitchingLemma2
set_option maxHeartbeats 800000 in
open Classical
open Classical SwitchingLemma2

namespace BernoulliCost
variable {n : ℕ}
def binomialPMF (nn : ℕ) (p : ℝ) (k : ℕ) : ℝ :=
  ↑(nn.choose k) * p ^ k * (1 - p) ^ (nn - k)
end BernoulliCost

namespace SwitchingLemma2
variable {n : ℕ}
abbrev Restriction (n : ℕ) := Fin n → Option Bool
end SwitchingLemma2

namespace SwitchingLemma2.Restriction
variable {n : ℕ}
def freeVars {n : ℕ} (ρ : Restriction n) : Finset (Fin n) :=
  Finset.univ.filter (fun i => (ρ i).isNone)
end SwitchingLemma2.Restriction

namespace BernoulliCost
variable {n : ℕ}
def fixedSizeRestrs (n k : ℕ) : Finset (Restriction n) :=
  Finset.univ.filter (fun ρ : Restriction n => ρ.freeVars.card = k)
end BernoulliCost

namespace SwitchingLemma2
variable {n : ℕ}
def bernoulliRestrWeight (p : ℝ) (ρ : Restriction n) : ℝ :=
  p ^ ρ.freeVars.card * ((1 - p) / 2) ^ (n - ρ.freeVars.card)
end SwitchingLemma2

namespace BernoulliCost
variable {n : ℕ}
def fixedSizeRestrProb (event : Restriction n → Prop) [DecidablePred event]
    (k : ℕ) : ℝ :=
  ↑((fixedSizeRestrs n k).filter (fun ρ => event ρ)).card / ↑(fixedSizeRestrs n k).card
end BernoulliCost

namespace SwitchingLemma2
variable {n : ℕ}
def bernoulliRestrProb (p : ℝ) (event : Restriction n → Prop)
    [DecidablePred event] : ℝ :=
  ∑ ρ : Restriction n,
    bernoulliRestrWeight p ρ * (if event ρ then 1 else 0)
end SwitchingLemma2

namespace BernoulliCost
variable {n : ℕ}
lemma bernoulli_decompose (p : ℝ) (hp : 0 ≤ p) (hp1 : p ≤ 1)
    (event : Restriction n → Prop) [DecidablePred event] :
    bernoulliRestrProb p event =
    ∑ k ∈ Finset.range (n + 1),
      binomialPMF n p k * fixedSizeRestrProb event k := sorry
end BernoulliCost
title: Decomposition of Bernoulli probability by free-variable count
statement_informal:
Decomposition of Bernoulli probability by free-variable count. For $0 \le p \le 1$ and any event, the Bernoulli$(p)$ restriction probability decomposes
according to the number of free variables:
\[
  \Pr_{R_p}[\mathrm{event}] = \sum_{k=0}^{n} \mathrm{binomialPMF}\,n\,p\,k \cdot \mathrm{fixedSizeRestrProb}\,(\mathrm{event})\,k,
\]
because conditioning $R_p$ on having exactly $k$ free variables yields exactly the model $R_k$.

definitions.title: Binomial probability mass function
definitions.informal: The binomial probability mass function, $\Pr[\mathrm{Bin}(n,p) = k] = \binom{n}{k}\, p^k\, (1-p)^{n-k}$.

definitions.title: Restriction
definitions.informal:
A restriction on $n$ variables is a map $\rho : \mathrm{Fin}\,n \to \mathrm{Option}\,\mathrm{Bool}$,
assigning each variable either a fixed Boolean value or leaving it free (none).

definitions.title: Free variables of a restriction
definitions.informal:
The set of free variables of a restriction $\rho$ is the finite set of indices
$i \in \mathrm{Fin}\,n$ for which $\rho(i)$ is unset (none).

definitions.title: Restrictions with a fixed number of free variables
definitions.informal:
For natural numbers $n$ and $k$, this is the finite set of restrictions on $n$ variables
whose set of free variables has cardinality exactly $k$.

definitions.title: Bernoulli weight of a restriction
definitions.informal:
For a parameter $p \in \bbr$ and a restriction $\rho$ on $n$ variables, the Bernoulli
weight is
\[
  p^{\abs{\mathrm{freeVars}(\rho)}} \cdot \left(\tfrac{1-p}{2}\right)^{\,n - \abs{\mathrm{freeVars}(\rho)}},
\]
so each free variable contributes a factor $p$ and each fixed variable a factor
$(1-p)/2$.

definitions.title: Fixed-size restriction probability $R_k$
definitions.informal:
The probability of an event under the fixed-size restriction model $R_k$, which is uniform
over restrictions with exactly $k$ free variables. It is defined as the fraction of
restrictions with exactly $k$ free variables that satisfy the event.

definitions.title: Bernoulli event probability
definitions.informal:
For a parameter $p$ and a (decidable) predicate $\mathrm{event}$ on restrictions, the
probability that the predicate holds under a Bernoulli($p$) random restriction is the
sum over all restrictions $\rho$ of $\mathrm{bernoulliRestrWeight}(p,\rho)$ weighted by
the indicator of $\mathrm{event}(\rho)$.

informal_statement:
Definitions:
- Restriction. A restriction on $n$ variables is a map $\rho : \mathrm{Fin}\,n \to \mathrm{Option}\,\mathrm{Bool}$,
assigning each variable either a fixed Boolean value or leaving it free (none).
- Free variables of a restriction. The set of free variables of a restriction $\rho$ is the finite set of indices
$i \in \mathrm{Fin}\,n$ for which $\rho(i)$ is unset (none).
- Restrictions with a fixed number of free variables. For natural numbers $n$ and $k$, this is the finite set of restrictions on $n$ variables
whose set of free variables has cardinality exactly $k$.
- Bernoulli weight of a restriction. For a parameter $p \in \bbr$ and a restriction $\rho$ on $n$ variables, the Bernoulli
weight is
\[
  p^{\abs{\mathrm{freeVars}(\rho)}} \cdot \left(\tfrac{1-p}{2}\right)^{\,n - \abs{\mathrm{freeVars}(\rho)}},
\]
so each free variable contributes a factor $p$ and each fixed variable a factor
$(1-p)/2$.
- Fixed-size restriction probability $R_k$. The probability of an event under the fixed-size restriction model $R_k$, which is uniform
over restrictions with exactly $k$ free variables. It is defined as the fraction of
restrictions with exactly $k$ free variables that satisfy the event.
- Bernoulli event probability. For a parameter $p$ and a (decidable) predicate $\mathrm{event}$ on restrictions, the
probability that the predicate holds under a Bernoulli($p$) random restriction is the

Statement: Bernoulli restriction cost, asymptotic version. Let $0 < p \le 1$, $w, s > 0$, and $\varepsilon > 0$. Then there exists $N$ such that for every
$n \ge N$ with $n > 0$ and every event satisfying
$\mathrm{fixedSizeRestrProb}\,(\mathrm{event})\,k \le (5kw/n)^s$ for all $k \le n$, one has
$\Pr_{R_p}[\mathrm{event}] \le (10pw)^s + \varepsilon$. Asymptotically the exponential tail
vanishes, leaving only the leading bound $(10pw)^s$.
formal_statement:
import Mathlib

open Classical
open SwitchingLemma2
set_option maxHeartbeats 800000 in
open Classical SwitchingLemma2

namespace SwitchingLemma2
variable {n : ℕ}
abbrev Restriction (n : ℕ) := Fin n → Option Bool
end SwitchingLemma2

namespace SwitchingLemma2.Restriction
variable {n : ℕ}
def freeVars {n : ℕ} (ρ : Restriction n) : Finset (Fin n) :=
  Finset.univ.filter (fun i => (ρ i).isNone)
end SwitchingLemma2.Restriction

namespace BernoulliCost
variable {n : ℕ}
def fixedSizeRestrs (n k : ℕ) : Finset (Restriction n) :=
  Finset.univ.filter (fun ρ : Restriction n => ρ.freeVars.card = k)
end BernoulliCost

namespace SwitchingLemma2
variable {n : ℕ}
def bernoulliRestrWeight (p : ℝ) (ρ : Restriction n) : ℝ :=
  p ^ ρ.freeVars.card * ((1 - p) / 2) ^ (n - ρ.freeVars.card)
end SwitchingLemma2

namespace BernoulliCost
variable {n : ℕ}
def fixedSizeRestrProb (event : Restriction n → Prop) [DecidablePred event]
    (k : ℕ) : ℝ :=
  ↑((fixedSizeRestrs n k).filter (fun ρ => event ρ)).card / ↑(fixedSizeRestrs n k).card
end BernoulliCost

namespace SwitchingLemma2
variable {n : ℕ}
def bernoulliRestrProb (p : ℝ) (event : Restriction n → Prop)
    [DecidablePred event] : ℝ :=
  ∑ ρ : Restriction n,
    bernoulliRestrWeight p ρ * (if event ρ then 1 else 0)
end SwitchingLemma2

namespace BernoulliCost
variable {n : ℕ}
theorem bernoulli_restriction_asymptotic
    (p : ℝ) (hp : 0 < p) (hp1 : p ≤ 1)
    (w s : ℕ) (hw : 0 < w) (hs : 0 < s)
    (ε : ℝ) (hε : 0 < ε) :
    ∃ N : ℕ, ∀ nn : ℕ, N ≤ nn → 0 < nn →
    ∀ (event : Restriction nn → Prop) [DecidablePred event],
    (∀ k : ℕ, k ≤ nn →
      fixedSizeRestrProb event k ≤ (5 * ↑k * ↑w / ↑nn) ^ s) →
    bernoulliRestrProb p event ≤ (10 * p * ↑w) ^ s + ε := sorry
end BernoulliCost
title: Bernoulli restriction cost, asymptotic version
statement_informal:
Bernoulli restriction cost, asymptotic version. Let $0 < p \le 1$, $w, s > 0$, and $\varepsilon > 0$. Then there exists $N$ such that for every
$n \ge N$ with $n > 0$ and every event satisfying
$\mathrm{fixedSizeRestrProb}\,(\mathrm{event})\,k \le (5kw/n)^s$ for all $k \le n$, one has
$\Pr_{R_p}[\mathrm{event}] \le (10pw)^s + \varepsilon$. Asymptotically the exponential tail
vanishes, leaving only the leading bound $(10pw)^s$.

set_option maxHeartbeats 800000 in
open Classical SwitchingLemma2

namespace SwitchingLemma2
variable {n : ℕ}

end SwitchingLemma2

namespace SwitchingLemma2.Restriction
variable {n : ℕ}

namespace SwitchingLemma2

namespace SwitchingLemma2

def bernoulliRestrProb (p : ℝ) (event : Restriction n → Prop)
    [DecidablePred event] : ℝ :=

namespace BernoulliCost
variable {n : ℕ}
theorem bernoulli_restriction_cost
    (n_pos : 0 < n) (p : ℝ) (hp : 0 < p) (hp1 : p ≤ 1)
    (w s : ℕ) (hw : 0 < w) (_hs : 0 < s)
    (event : Restriction n → Prop) [DecidablePred event]
    (h_fixed : ∀ k : ℕ, k ≤ n →

Definitions:
- Binomial probability mass function. The binomial probability mass function, $\Pr[\mathrm{Bin}(n,p) = k] = \binom{n}{k}\, p^k\, (1-p)^{n-k}$.

Statement: Binomial PMF is nonnegative. If $0 \le p \le 1$, then $0 \le \mathrm{binomialPMF}\,n\,p\,k$ for every $k$.
formal_statement:
import Mathlib

open SwitchingLemma2
set_option maxHeartbeats 800000 in

namespace BernoulliCost
variable {n : ℕ}
def binomialPMF (nn : ℕ) (p : ℝ) (k : ℕ) : ℝ :=
  ↑(nn.choose k) * p ^ k * (1 - p) ^ (nn - k)
end BernoulliCost

namespace BernoulliCost
variable {n : ℕ}
lemma binomialPMF_nonneg (p : ℝ) (hp : 0 ≤ p) (hp1 : p ≤ 1) (k : ℕ) :
    0 ≤ binomialPMF n p k := sorry
end BernoulliCost
title: Binomial PMF is nonnegative
statement_informal: Binomial PMF is nonnegative. If $0 \le p \le 1$, then $0 \le \mathrm{binomialPMF}\,n\,p\,k$ for every $k$.

definitions.title: Binomial probability mass function
definitions.informal: The binomial probability mass function, $\Pr[\mathrm{Bin}(n,p) = k] = \binom{n}{k}\, p^k\, (1-p)^{n-k}$.

informal_statement:
Definitions:
- Binomial probability mass function. The binomial probability mass function, $\Pr[\mathrm{Bin}(n,p) = k] = \binom{n}{k}\, p^k\, (1-p)^{n-k}$.

Statement: Binomial PMF sums to one. If $0 \le p \le 1$, then the binomial PMF sums to $1$ over $k$ ranging in $\{0, \dots, n\}$,
i.e.\ $\sum_{k=0}^{n} \mathrm{binomialPMF}\,n\,p\,k = 1$. This is the binomial theorem
applied to $(p + (1-p))^n = 1$.
formal_statement:
import Mathlib

open SwitchingLemma2
set_option maxHeartbeats 800000 in

namespace BernoulliCost
variable {n : ℕ}
def binomialPMF (nn : ℕ) (p : ℝ) (k : ℕ) : ℝ :=
  ↑(nn.choose k) * p ^ k * (1 - p) ^ (nn - k)
end BernoulliCost

namespace BernoulliCost
variable {n : ℕ}
lemma binomialPMF_sum_one (p : ℝ) (hp : 0 ≤ p) (hp1 : p ≤ 1) :
    ∑ k ∈ Finset.range (n + 1), binomialPMF n p k = 1 := sorry
end BernoulliCost
title: Binomial PMF sums to one
statement_informal:
Binomial PMF sums to one. If $0 \le p \le 1$, then the binomial PMF sums to $1$ over $k$ ranging in $\{0, \dots, n\}$,
i.e.\ $\sum_{k=0}^{n} \mathrm{binomialPMF}\,n\,p\,k = 1$. This is the binomial theorem
applied to $(p + (1-p))^n = 1$.

definitions.title: Binomial probability mass function
definitions.informal: The binomial probability mass function, $\Pr[\mathrm{Bin}(n,p) = k] = \binom{n}{k}\, p^k\, (1-p)^{n-k}$.

informal_statement:
Definitions:
- Binomial probability mass function. The binomial probability mass function, $\Pr[\mathrm{Bin}(n,p) = k] = \binom{n}{k}\, p^k\, (1-p)^{n-k}$.

Statement: Chernoff upper-tail bound for the binomial distribution. For $0 < p \le 1$, the upper tail of the binomial distribution beyond $2np$ is exponentially
small: summing $\mathrm{binomialPMF}\,n\,p\,k$ over those $k \in \{0, \dots, n\}$ with $k > 2np$
is at most $e^{-np/3}$.
formal_statement:
import Mathlib

open SwitchingLemma2
set_option maxHeartbeats 800000 in

namespace BernoulliCost
variable {n : ℕ}
def binomialPMF (nn : ℕ) (p : ℝ) (k : ℕ) : ℝ :=
  ↑(nn.choose k) * p ^ k * (1 - p) ^ (nn - k)
end BernoulliCost

namespace BernoulliCost
variable {n : ℕ}
lemma chernoff_binomial_upper_tail (nn : ℕ) (p : ℝ) (hp : 0 < p) (hp1 : p ≤ 1) :
    ∑ k ∈ (Finset.range (nn + 1)).filter (fun k : ℕ => (↑k : ℝ) > 2 * ↑nn * p),
      binomialPMF nn p k ≤ Real.exp (-(↑nn * p / 3)) := sorry
end BernoulliCost
title: Chernoff upper-tail bound for the binomial distribution
statement_informal:
Chernoff upper-tail bound for the binomial distribution. For $0 < p \le 1$, the upper tail of the binomial distribution beyond $2np$ is exponentially
small: summing $\mathrm{binomialPMF}\,n\,p\,k$ over those $k \in \{0, \dots, n\}$ with $k > 2np$
is at most $e^{-np/3}$.

definitions.title: Binomial probability mass function
definitions.informal: The binomial probability mass function, $\Pr[\mathrm{Bin}(n,p) = k] = \binom{n}{k}\, p^k\, (1-p)^{n-k}$.

informal_statement:
Eventual smallness of the exponential tail. For any $p > 0$ and any $\varepsilon > 0$, there exists $N$ such that for all $m \ge N$ one has
$e^{-mp/3} < \varepsilon$; that is, the tail term decays to $0$.

open SwitchingLemma2
set_option maxHeartbeats 800000 in

namespace BernoulliCost
variable {n : ℕ}
lemma exp_neg_eventually_small (p : ℝ) (hp : 0 < p) (ε : ℝ) (hε : 0 < ε) :
    ∃ N : ℕ, ∀ m : ℕ, N ≤ m →
      Real.exp (-(↑m * p / 3)) < ε := sorry
end BernoulliCost
title: Eventual smallness of the exponential tail
statement_informal:
Eventual smallness of the exponential tail. For any $p > 0$ and any $\varepsilon > 0$, there exists $N$ such that for all $m \ge N$ one has
$e^{-mp/3} < \varepsilon$; that is, the tail term decays to $0$.

over restrictions with exactly $k$ free variables. It is defined as the fraction of
